# Apache Kafka Connector - Full Documentation

# Apache Kafka Connector 4.14

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Connector for Apache Kafka (Apache Kafka Connector) enables you to interact with the Apache
Kafka messaging system and achieve seamless integration between your Mule app and a Kafka cluster,
using Mule runtime engine (Mule).

For compatibility information, see the Apache Kafka Connector Release Notes.

## Before You Begin

Before creating an app, you must:

- Have access to the Apache Kafka target resource and Anypoint Platform
- Understand how to create a Mule app using Anypoint Studio
- Have access to Apache Kafka to get values for the fields that appear in Studio

## Common Use Cases for the Connector

Common use cases for Apache Kafka Connector include:

- **Log Aggregation**

  Leverage the low-latency processing from Apache Kafka to collect logs from multiple services and
  make them available in a standard format to multiple consumers. For example, you can publish a
  message to Apache Kafka and then retrieve it.

- **Analysis and Metrics**

  Optimize your advertising budget by integrating Apache Kafka and your big data analytics solution
  to analyze end user activity, such as page views, clicks, shares, and so on, to serve relevant
  ads. For example, a department store can use a website activity tracker to improve the online
  shopping experience. The gathered data is sent to multiple departments for various computations.
  Each department reviews the received information to stay informed about what the customer is
  looking for and then provides recommendations accordingly.

- **Notifications and Alerts**

  Notify customers about various financial events, such as the recent amount of a transaction or
  more complex events like future investment suggestions based on integrations with credit agencies,
  location, and so on.

- **Time-Sensitive Applications**

  Build integration applications that are time-sensitive. For example, you can build an application
  that ensures that patients in a hospital receive the care they need in a timely manner with a
  hospital server that processes emergency requests for patient admissions and orders the requests
  based on priority determined by specified criteria. In this scenario, an application processes
  Apache Kafka messages in the order that they are received, relying on the order and idempotency of
  the messages sent through the queue. For example, a newsroom can use the Apache Kafka system to
  deliver the latest news. Retrieve the latest news by building an integration application by
  reading from the end of the Apache Kafka queue first.

For examples of these use cases, refer to
[Apache Kafka Connector Examples](kafka-connector-examples).

### Publish-to Use Cases

With Kafka Connector, applications can publish binary messages to the Kafka server. Some examples of
use cases include:

- Publishing binary data to the Kafka server and the underlying topic.
- Setting the batch size of the data to publish to Kafka when working with large volumes of data.
  - To optimize for performance, companies can batch messages to send. Kafka stores the message
    until the batch size is reached and completes the send at that time.

- Configuring the message size to send to focus on optimal performance.
  - Kafka is not intended to process and handle large messages. Messages are typically in the 1 MB
    range and users set the size to avoid performance degradation. In the case of an error, the
    message is rejected.

- Blocking further commits on a configurable level of acknowledgment for the messages published to
  the Kafka server to ensure successful data transmission.
  - Acknowledgments enable users to ensure that the message is sent prior to publishing more
    messages. This is specifically useful in time-ordered or event-ordered messages, such as logs or
    patient drug administration orders. Ensuring that messages are sent in order helps the
    downstream application process the messages in the right order.

- Configuring how data is serialized during a publish.
- Securing messages sent during Mutual TLS.

The following lists the acceptance criteria for publishing:

- Publish a message to a Kafka topic.
- Configure the batch size and size of the message.
- Obtain an acknowledgment once publishing is complete at the configured level.
- Send the message securely via Mutual TLS.
- Configure the serialization type.

### Subscribe-from Use Cases

With Kafka Connector, applications can obtain messages from Kafka for further consumption
downstream. Some examples of use cases include:

Reading from Kafka:

- Subscribing to the Kafka server and the underlying group to read and consume messages for
  downstream processing.
- Subscribing to a specific Kafka partition to process messages.
  - Users with a high availability microservice donât want to rely on Kafkaâs rebalancing in the
    event of a failure.
  - If users have a local state associated with their partition, they want to process only that
    partition.

- Configuring the offset value to a set value or to the beginning or end of the topic.
  - Users have a local store and want to use that information to start processing their records.
  - Users have time-sensitive records and want to skip to the end if the application must be
    up-to-date or skip to the beginning if it is a new application processing the records.

- Reading the offset value from an alternate storage system on restart.
  - Users have a local store with the offset information and want to read the value from their local
    storage.

- Setting the interval for which to block the receipt of messages for varying downstream processing
  intervals.

Processing the messages:

- Ensuring there is no data loss when the downstream application crashes by manually configuring
  commits to have more control.
- Storing a commit value in a local store or in the Kafka storage and blocking until the store is
  successful.
- Storing an offset asynchronously in a local store or in the Kafka storage.
- Using Kafkaâs in-built capability of load-balancing with multiple applications reading from the
  same group ID.
- Configuring the number of records to process in a single call.

The following lists the acceptance criteria for subscribing:

- Read a message from Kafka from a specific partition or topic.
- Read a message from Kafka after obtaining the offset from a local store.
- Read a message from the beginning of the queue, the end of the queue, and a pre-specified offset.
- Ensure a high level of performance when there is one consumer per thread. Multiple consumers exist
  in a single group.

## Audience

- Starting user

  To create a Mule app, see [Apache Kafka Studio Configuration](kafka-connector-studio).

- Power user

  Read [XML and Maven Support](kafka-connector-xml-maven) and [Examples](kafka-connector-examples).

## Next

After you complete the prerequisites, you are ready to create an app with
[Anypoint Studio](kafka-connector-studio).

# Apache Kafka Connector 4.14 - Additional Configuration Information

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Set additional configuration for Anypoint Connector for Apache Kafka (Apache Kafka Connector).

## Configure a Custom Partitioner

You can configure a custom partitioner for Apache Kafka Connector in a Mule app:

1.  Use Maven to create a Java project and add the custom partitioner to it:

    ```java
    public class CustomPartitioner implements Partitioner {
    // Implementation
    }
    ```

2.  Add this dependency in the Mule app:

    ```java
    <dependencies>
     <dependency>
       <groupId>org.example</groupId>
       <artifactId>custom-partitioner</artifactId>
       <version>1.0-SNAPSHOT</version>
     </dependency>
    </dependencies>
    ```

3.  Add this dependency to `additionalPluginDependencies`:

    ```java
    <plugin>
     <groupId>org.mule.tools.maven</groupId>
     <artifactId>mule-maven-plugin</artifactId>
     <version>${mule.maven.plugin.version}</version>
     <extensions>true</extensions>
     <configuration>
      <additionalPluginDependencies>
      <plugin>
       <groupId>com.mulesoft.connectors</groupId>
       <artifactId>mule-kafka-connector</artifactId>
        <additionalDependencies>
         <dependency>
          <groupId>io.confluent</groupId>
          <artifactId>kafka-avro-serializer</artifactId>
          <version>7.6.1</version>
         </dependency>
         <dependency>
          <groupId>org.example</groupId>
          <artifactId>custom-partitioner</artifactId>
          <version>1.0-SNAPSHOT</version>
         </dependency>
        </additionalDependencies>
       </plugin>
       </additionalPluginDependencies>
       <classifier>mule-application</classifier>
     </configuration>
    </plugin>
    ```

4.  Add the custom partitioner to the producer configuration:

    ```java
    <kafka:producer-config name="Apache_Kafka_Producer_configuration"
        doc:name="Apache Kafka Producer configuration"
        doc:id="cb9de3c5-d6d2-4f9b-a255-75a9985bffb7">
        <kafka:producer-plaintext-connection>
            <kafka:bootstrap-servers>
                <kafka:bootstrap-server value="<kafka-url>" />
            </kafka:bootstrap-servers>
            <kafka:additional-properties>
                <kafka:additional-property key="key.serializer"
                    value="org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer" />
                <kafka:additional-property key="value.serializer"
                    value="com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroSerializer" />
                <kafka:additional-property key="schema.registry.url"
                    value="<schema-registry-url>" />
                <kafka:additional-property key="basic.auth.credentials.source"
                    value="USER_INFO" />
                <kafka:additional-property key="basic.auth.user.info"
                    value="user:pass" />
                <kafka:additional-property key="auto.register.schemas"
                    value="true" />
                <kafka:additional-property key="value.subject.name.strategy"
                    value="io.confluent.kafka.serializers.subject.TopicNameStrategy" />
                <kafka:additional-property key="partitioner.class"
                    value="org.example.CustomPartitioner" />
            </kafka:additional-properties>
        </kafka:producer-plaintext-connection>
    </kafka:producer-config>
    ```

## Next Step

After you complete configuring the connector, you can try the [examples](kafka-connector-examples).

## See Also

- [Introduction to Anypoint Connectors](../../connectors/introduction/introduction-to-anypoint-connectors)
- [Salesforce Help](https://help.salesforce.com)

# Integrate Confluent Schema Registry with Kafka Connector - Example

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The following example shows how to configure the Confluent Schema Registry using Kafka Connector.

Imagine you are developing a Mule 4 app that processes streaming data from various sources. To
ensure the data conforms to defined schemas and to manage schema evolution effectively, you
integrate Confluent Schema Registry with your Kafka Connector. This setup is crucial for developers
who need to enforce data integrity and compatibility across different versions of data producers and
consumers.

The configuration consists of two parts:

- Configuring the Confluent Schema Registry in the Kakfa Connector connection using the **Additional
  Properties** field for the **Consumer** operation.
- Configuring the Kafka Avro dependency in the POM file of the Mule app

## Configure the Confluent Schema Registry in the Kakfa Connector Connection

Use the **Additional Properties** field to configure the Confluent Schema Registry.

### Configure the Consumer Operation

To configure the **Consumer** operation in Anypoint Studio:

1.  Open your Mule project in Anypoint Studio and navigate to the **Global Elements** tab.
2.  Click **Create**.
3.  In the search box, type `kafka`.
4.  Select **Apache Kafka Consumer configuration**.
5.  Click **OK**.
6.  In the configuration screen under connection, select **Advanced**.
7.  Fill in all the required connection parameters.
8.  For **Additional properties**, select **Edit inline**.
9.  Click the plus sign to add a new additional property.
10. Set **Key** to `key.deserializer` and **Value** to
    `com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroDeserializer`.
11. Repeat step 9 to add all the required Confluent Schema Registry properties.

Optionally, you can configure these system properties `maxCacheSize`, `maxCacheTTL` for the key
`mule.kafka.muleKafkaAvroDeserializer`:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Property</th><th>Description</th><th>Default</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong><code>mule.kafka.muleKafkaAvroDeserializer.maxCacheSize</code></strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum number of schemas to store in cache.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>200</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong><code>mule.kafka.muleKafkaAvroDeserializer.maxCacheTTL</code></strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>The time-to-live (TTL) in minutes that a schema remains in cache when not being used.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>30 minutes</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

In the **Configuration XML** window, check the complete confluent schema registry configuration
under the `<kafka:additional-property>` tags:

```xml
<kafka:consumer-config name="Apache_Kafka_Consumer_configurationâ >
<kafka:consumer-plaintext-connection groupId="JenkinsTest">
<kafka:bootstrap-servers >
	<kafka:bootstrap-server value="localhost:9092" />
	</kafka:bootstrap-servers>
<kafka:additional-properties >
  <kafka:additional-property key="key.deserializer" value="com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroDeserializer" />
  <kafka:additional-property key="value.deserializer" value="com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroDeserializer" />
       <kafka:additional-property key="schema.registry.url" value="https://example.us-east-2.aws.confluent.cloud" />
      <kafka:additional-property key="basic.auth.credentials.source" value="USER_INFO" />
      <kafka:additional-property key="basic.auth.user.info" value="<put your credentials here>" />
       <kafka:additional-property key="auto.register.schemas" value="false" />
     <kafka:additional-property key="value.subject.name.strategy" value="io.confluent.kafka.serializers.subject.TopicNameStrategy" />
        </kafka:additional-properties>
        <kafka:topic-patterns >
		<kafka:topic-pattern value="InfraTopic" />
	    </kafka:topic-patterns>
        </kafka:consumer-plaintext-connection>
	    </kafka:consumer-config>
```

## Configure the Kafka Avro dependency in the POM File

In the POM file of the Mule app, configure the Kafka Avro dependency that contains the class to use
to serialize and deserialize. You must update the `<dependencies>`, `<plugin>`, and `<repository>`
sections of the POM file.

### Configure the <dependencies> Section

In the `<dependencies>` section, set `kafka-avro-serializer`:

```xml
<dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>io.confluent</groupId>
            <artifactId>kafka-avro-serializer</artifactId>
            <version>7.4.0</version>
        </dependency>
</dependencies>
```

### Configure the <plugin> Section

In the `<plugin>` section, set `kafka-avro-serializer` as a `<sharedLibrary>`:

```xml
<plugin>
	<groupId>org.mule.tools.maven</groupId>
	<artifactId>mule-maven-plugin</artifactId>
	<version>${mule.maven.plugin.version}</version>
		<extensions>true</extensions>
				<configuration>
					<additionalPluginDependencies>
						<plugin>
							<groupId>com.mulesoft.connectors</groupId>
							<artifactId>mule-kafka-connector</artifactId>
							<additionalDependencies>
								<dependency>
									<groupId>io.confluent</groupId>
									<artifactId>kafka-avro-serializer</artifactId>
									<version>7.6.1</version>
								</dependency>
							</additionalDependencies>
						</plugin>
					</additionalPluginDependencies>
					<sharedLibraries>
						<sharedLibrary>
							<groupId>io.confluent</groupId>
							<artifactId>kafka-avro-serializer</artifactId>
						</sharedLibrary>
					</sharedLibraries>
					<classifier>mule-application</classifier>
   </configuration>
</plugin>
```

Then, set the `avro-maven-plugin` plugin. This dependency from the `org.apache.avro` group is a
plugin for Apache Maven that facilitates handling Avro schema files in Java projects. The plugin
automatically generates Java classes from Avro schemas (.avsc), protocols (.avpr), and IDL files
(.avdl). This process simplifies development by eliminating the need to manually write Java classes
that represent Avro schemas, ensuring that the Java code is always synchronized with the Avro
schemas.

The `<sourceDirectory>` specifices the folder where the Avro schemas live, for example
`<sourceDirectory>${project.basedir}/PATH_TO_SCHEMAS</sourceDirectory>`.

```xml
<plugin>
<groupId>org.apache.avro</groupId>
	<artifactId>avro-maven-plugin</artifactId>
	<version>1.10.2</version>
	<executions>
		<execution>
			<phase>generate-sources</phase>
			<goals>
				<goal>schema</goal>
			</goals>
			<configuration>
            <sourceDirectory>${project.basedir}/PATH_TO_SCHEMAS</sourceDirectory>
			</configuration>
		</execution>
	</executions>
</plugin>
```

### Configure the <repository> Section

In the `<repository>` section, configure the repository of the dependency:

```xml
<repository>
            <id>confluent</id>
            <url>https://packages.confluent.io/maven/</url>
 </repository>
```

## See Also

- [Kafka Connector Examples](kafka-connector-examples)
- [Salesforce Help](https://help.salesforce.com)

# Apache Kafka 4.14 Examples

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The following examples shows how to configure Apache Kafka Connector:

- [Publish and Consume Example](kafka-connector-publish-consume-example)  
  Use the **Publish** and **Consume** to publish a message to Apache Kafka and then retrieve it.
- [Integrate Confluent Schema Registry](kafka-connector-configure-confluent-example)  
  Configure Confluent Schema Registry via the **Additional Properties** field using Kafka Connector.

# Publish and Then Consume a Topic with Kafka Connector

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This example shows how to use two Apache Kafka Connector operations, **Publish** and **Consume**, to
publish a message to Apache Kafka and then retrieve it. The example contains two flows:

- The first flow is the Producer flow, which publishes a message to Apache Kafka.
- The second flow is the Consumer flow, which consumes a message from Apache Kafka.

![Apache Kafka Connector Producer flow in Studio](_images/kafka-producer-studio-flow.png)

![Apache Kafka Connector Consumer flow in Studio](_images/kafka-consumer-studio-flow.png)

## Configure the First Flow

Configure the first flow that publishes a message to Apache Kafka. Creating the first flow involves
configuring a **Listener** component, a **Logger** component, a **Publish** operation, and a **Set
Payload** transformer.

### Configure the HTTP Listener

Configure the **Listener** component to initiate a Mule flow when a call is made to the
`/pushMessage` path:

1.  Create a new Mule project in Studio.
2.  From the **Mule Palette** view, select **HTTP** and drag the **Listener** component to the
    canvas.
3.  In the properties window, click **+** next to the **Connector configuration** field to add a
    global element.
4.  Accept the defaults.
5.  In the properties window, set the **Path** field value to `/pushMessage`.

### Add the Logger Component

Add the **Logger** component to display the response in the Mule console:

1.  From the **Mule Palette** view, select **Core** and drag a **Logger** component to the right of
    **Listener**.
2.  In the properties window, configure the following fields:

    <table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Value</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Display Name</p></td><td><p>Name for the logger.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message</p></td><td><p><code>#["Message: '" payload.message "' is going to be published to topic: '" payload.topic "'."]</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Level</p></td><td><p>INFO (Default)</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

    The following image shows an example of the **Logger** configuration in the properties window:

    ![Logger properties window configuration](_images/kafka-logger-config-publish.png)

### Add the Publish Operation

Add the **Publish** operation to publish a message to the specified Kafka topic:

1.  From the **Mule Palette** view, select **Apache Kafka** and drag the **Publish** operation to
    the right of **Logger**.
2.  In the properties window, click **+** next to the **Connector configuration** field to add a
    global element.
3.  Configure the global element as follows:
    1.  In the **Bootstrap Server URLs** field, select **Edit inline** and then click the green plus
        sign.
    2.  Enter the value `${config.basic.bootstrapServers}` and click **Finish**.

4.  In the properties window, configure the following fields:

    <table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Value</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Display Name</p></td><td><p>Name for the connector operation.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Connector configuration</p></td><td><p>Global configuration you just created.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Topic</p></td><td><p><code>\#[payload.topic]</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Key</p></td><td><p><code>#[now()]</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message</p></td><td><p><code>payload</code></p></td></tr></tbody></table>

    The following image shows an example of the **Publish** configuration in the properties window:

    ![Publish properties window configuration](_images/kafka-publish.png)

### Add the Set Payload Transformer

Add the **Set Payload** transformer to push the response builder:

1.  From the **Mule Palette** view, select **Core** and drag the **Set Payload** transformer to the
    right of **Publish**.
2.  In the properties window, configure the following fields:

    <table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Value</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Display Name</p></td><td><p>Name for the transformer, such as <code>Push response builder</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Value</p></td><td><p><code>Message successfully sent to Apache Kafka topic.</code></p></td></tr></tbody></table>

    The following image shows an example of the **Set Payload** configuration in the properties
    window:

    ![Set Payload properties window configuration](_images/kafka-set-payload.png)

## Configure the Second Flow

Configure the second flow that consumes a message from Apache Kafka. Creating the second flow
involves configuring a **Message listener** source and a **Logger** component.

### Add the Message Listener Source

Add the **Message listener** source to consume a message endpoint:

1.  From the **Mule Palette** view, select **Apache Kafka** and drag the **Message listener** source
    to the canvas.
2.  In the properties window, click **+** next to the **Connector configuration** field to add a
    global element.
3.  Configure the global element as follows:
    1.  In the **Bootstrap Server URLs** field, select **Edit inline** and then click the green plus
        sign.
    2.  Enter the value `${config.basic.bootstrapServers}` and click **Finish.**
    3.  In the **Group ID** field, enter `${consumer.groupId}`.
    4.  In the **Topic Subscription Patterns** field, select **Edit inline** and then click the
        green plus sign.
    5.  Enter the value `${topic1}` and click **Finish**.

4.  In the properties window, configure the following fields:

    <table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Value</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Display Name</p></td><td><p>Name for the connector source.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Connector configuration</p></td><td><p>Global configuration you just created.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

    The following image shows an example of the **Message listener** configuration in the properties
    window:

    ![Message listener properties window configuration](_images/kafka-message-listener-config.png)

### Add the Logger Component

Add the **Logger** component to display the response in the Mule console:

1.  From the **Mule Palette** view, select **Core** and drag a **Logger** component to the right of
    **Message listener**.
2.  In the properties window, configure the following fields:

    <table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Value</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Display Name</p></td><td><p>Name for the logger.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message</p></td><td><p><code>"New message arrived: " payload ", key:" attributes.key ", partition:" attributes.partition ", offset:" ++ attributes.offset</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Level</p></td><td><p>INFO (Default)</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

    The following image shows an example of the **Logger** configuration in the properties window:

    ![Logger properties window configuration](_images/kafka-logger-config-consume.png)

## XML for This Example

Paste this code into the Studio XML editor to quickly load the flow for this example into your Mule
app:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>

<mule xmlns:ee="http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/ee/core"
xmlns:Kafka="http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/Kafka"
	xmlns:http="http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/http"
	xmlns="http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/core"
	xmlns:doc="http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/documentation"
	xmlns:spring="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans"
	xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
	xsi:schemaLocation="
http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/http http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/http/current/mule-http.xsd
http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/http
  http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/http/current/mule-http.xsd
  http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans
	http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans/spring-beans-current.xsd
	http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/core
	http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/core/current/mule.xsd
	http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/http
	http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/http/current/mule-http.xsd
	http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/ee/core
	http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/ee/core/current/mule-ee.xsd
	http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/kafka
	http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/kafka/current/mule-kafka.xsd">
  <configuration-properties file="mule-app.properties">
	</configuration-properties>
  <http:listener-config name="HTTP_Listener_config"
    doc:name="HTTP Listener config"  >
  <http:listener-connection host="0.0.0.0" port="8081" />
  </http:listener-config>

  <kafka:consumer-config name="Apache_Kafka_Consumer_configuration"
 	  doc:name="Apache Kafka Consumer configuration" >
	  <kafka:consumer-plaintext-connection groupId="${consumer.groupId}" >
	  <kafka:bootstrap-servers >
	    <kafka:bootstrap-server value="${config.basic.bootstrapServers}" />
	  </kafka:bootstrap-servers>
	  <kafka:topic-patterns >
	    <kafka:topic-pattern value='${topic1}
	    &lt;kafka:topic-pattern value="topic-1" /&gt;
	    &lt;kafka:topic-pattern value="topic-2" /&gt;
	    &lt;/kafka:topic-patterns&gt;]' />
	  </kafka:topic-patterns>
	  </kafka:consumer-plaintext-connection>
	</kafka:consumer-config>
	<kafka:producer-config name="Apache_Kafka_Producer_configuration"
	  doc:name="Apache Kafka Producer configuration" >
	  <kafka:producer-plaintext-connection >
	  <kafka:bootstrap-servers >
	    <kafka:bootstrap-server value="${config.basic.bootstrapServers}" />
	  </kafka:bootstrap-servers>
	  </kafka:producer-plaintext-connection>
	  </kafka:producer-config>
	<kafka:consumer-config name="Apache_Kafka_Consumer_configuration1" doc:name="Apache Kafka Consumer configuration" >
		<kafka:consumer-plaintext-connection groupId="${consumer.groupId}" >
			<kafka:bootstrap-servers >
				<kafka:bootstrap-server value="${config.basic.bootstrapServers}" />
			</kafka:bootstrap-servers>
			<kafka:topic-patterns >
				<kafka:topic-pattern value="${config.topics}" />
			</kafka:topic-patterns>
		</kafka:consumer-plaintext-connection>
	</kafka:consumer-config>
	<flow name="Producer-Flow" >
		<http:listener doc:name="Push message endpoint" config-ref="HTTP_Listener_config" path="/pushMessage" />
		<logger level="INFO" doc:name="Logger" message="&amp;quot;Message: '&amp;quot; ++ payload.message ++ &amp;quot;' is going to be published to topic: '&amp;quot; ++ payload.topic ++ &amp;quot;'.&amp;quot;" />
		<kafka:publish doc:name="Producer" topic="#[payload.topic]" key="#[now()]" config-ref="Apache_Kafka_Producer_configuration" />
		<set-payload value="Message successfully sent to Apache Kafka topic." doc:name="Push response builder" />
	</flow>
	<flow name="Consumer-Flow" >
		<kafka:message-listener doc:name="Consume message endpoint" config-ref="Apache_Kafka_Consumer_configuration"/>
		<logger level="INFO" doc:name="Logger" message="'New message arrived: ' ++ payload ++ &amp;quot;, key:&amp;quot; ++ attributes.key ++ &amp;quot;, partition:&amp;quot; ++ attributes.partition ++ &amp;quot;, offset:&amp;quot; ++ attributes.offset"/>
	</flow>
</mule>
```

## See Also

- [Introduction to Anypoint Connectors](../../connectors/introduction/introduction-to-anypoint-connectors)
- [Salesforce Help](https://help.salesforce.com)
---

# Apache Kafka Connector 4.14 Reference

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Connector for Apache Kafka (Apache Kafka Connector) enables you to interact with the Apache
Kafka messaging system. It provides seamless integration between your Mule app and an Apache Kafka
cluster, using Mule runtime engine (Mule).

> [!NOTE] When a **Message Listener** or a **Batch Message Listener** uses the `MANUAL`
> acknowledgement mode, you must use a **Commit** operation at the end of the flow when the flow
> finishes successfully (either because there were no errors or because there is an **On Error
> Continue** component in the flow).

> [!NOTE] Mule server notifications are not supported because the Kafka library used in the
> connector manages network disruptions internally.

## Consumer Configuration

Configuration for consumers for Apache Kafka Connector.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Name</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Name for this configuration. Connectors reference the configuration with this name.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection</strong></p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#consumer-plaintext">Consumer Plaintext Connection</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#consumer-sasl-kerberos">Consumer Kerberos Connection</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#consumer-config_consumer-sasl-oauth-bearer-cc-connection">Consumer SASL/OAUTHBEARER - Client Credentials Connection</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#consumer-sasl-scram">Consumer SASL/SCRAM Connection</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#consumer-sasl-plain">Consumer SASL/PLAIN Connection</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#consumer-config_consumer-sasl-token-connection">Consumer SASL/TOKEN Connection</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Connection types for this configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default acknowledgement mode</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of</p></div><div><ul><li><p>AUTO</p></li><li><p>MANUAL</p></li><li><p>IMMEDIATE</p></li><li><p>DUPS_OK</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines the way that the Kafka Broker instance is notified of the consumption of messages.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>AUTO</p><div><p>Commits messages automatically if the flow finishes successfully.</p></div></li><li><p>MANUAL</p><div><p>The user must commit messages manually upon receipt by using the <strong>Commit</strong> operation.</p></div></li><li><p>IMMEDIATE</p><div><p>Mule automatically commits the messages upon receipt before triggering the flow.</p></div></li><li><p>DUPS_OK</p><div><p>Works in the same way as MANUAL, but the commits are asynchronous, which can lead to duplicate records.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>AUTO</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default listener poll timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time to wait to perform a poll if data is not available in the buffer (fetched). If no value is set, the poll is returned immediately with any records that are currently available in the buffer or else returns empty if there is no data. Must not be negative.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>100</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default listener poll timeout time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Default listener poll timeout</strong> field. This combines with <strong>Poll Timeout</strong> to define the total timeout for the polling.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MILLISECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default operation poll timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time to wait for an operation to finish. If no value is set or a negative value is set, the operation waits forever.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>-1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default operation poll timeout time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Default operation timeout</strong> field. This combines with <strong>Operation Timeout</strong> to define the total default timeout for the operations that use this configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Zone ID</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Converts the provided timestamps into <code>ZonedLocalDateTimes</code> in the results. The default value is provided by the system.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Expiration Policy</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#ExpirationPolicy">Expiration Policy</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configures the minimum amount of time that a dynamic configuration instance can remain idle before Mule considers it eligible for expiration.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Consumer Plaintext Connection Type

Use an unauthenticated and non-encrypted connection type.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Bootstrap Server URLs</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of servers to bootstrap the connection with the Kafka cluster. This can be a partial list of the available servers. For example, <code>localhost:9071</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Group ID</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Default group ID for all the Kafka consumers that use this configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Consumer Amount</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the number of consumers the connection initially creates.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum polling interval</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum delay between invocations of poll() when using consumer group management. This places an upper bound on the amount of time that the consumer can be idle before fetching more records. If poll() is not called before the expiration of this timeout, then the consumer is considered failed and the group rebalances to reassign the partitions to another member. For consumers using a non-null <code>group.instance.id</code> which reaches this timeout, partitions are not immediately reassigned. Instead, the consumer stops sending heartbeats and partitions are reassigned after the expiration of <code>session.timeout.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>60</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum Polling Interval Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Maximum polling interval</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Isolation Level</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>READ_UNCOMMITTED</p></li><li><p>READ_COMMITTED</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how to read messages that are written transactionally.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>READ_COMMITTED</code></p><div><p><code>consumer.poll()</code> returns only committed transactional messages are returned.</p></div></li><li><p><code>READ_UNCOMMITTED</code></p><div><p><code>consumer.poll()</code> returns all messages, even transactional messages that were aborted.</p></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Non-transactional messages are returned unconditionally in either mode. Messages are always returned in offset order. Hence, in the <code>READ_COMMITTED</code> mode, <code>consumer.poll()</code> returns only messages up to the last stable offset (LSO), which is one less than the offset of the first open transaction.</p></div><div><p>In particular, any messages appearing after messages belonging to ongoing transactions are withheld until the relevant transaction is completed. As a result, <code>READ_COMMITTED</code> consumers are not able to read up to the high watermark when there are in-flight transactions. Furthermore, when in <code>READ_COMMITTED</code>, the <code>seekToEnd</code> method returns the LSO.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>READ_UNCOMMITTED</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Exclude internal topics</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines whether internal topics matching a subscribed pattern are excluded from the subscription. It is always possible to explicitly subscribe to an internal topic.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>true</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Auto offset reset</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>EARLIEST</p></li><li><p>LATEST</p></li><li><p>ERROR</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines what to do when there is no initial offset in Kafka or if the current offset no longer exists on the server, for example, because the data was deleted.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>EARLIEST</p><div><p>Automatically reset the offset to the earliest offset.</p></div></li><li><p>LATEST</p><div><p>Automatically reset the offset to the latest offset.</p></div></li><li><p>ERROR</p><div><p>Throw an error if no previous offset is found for the consumerâs group.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>LATEST</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Amount of time to wait before attempting to retry a failed request to a given topic partition. This avoids repeatedly sending requests in a tight loop under some failure scenarios.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>100</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MILLISECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Check CRC</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Automatically check the CRC32 of the records consumed. This ensures that no on-the-wire or on-disk corruption to the messages occurred. This check adds some overhead, so in situations that require extremely high performance, this can be disabled.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>true</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP receive buffer (SO_RCVBUF) to use when reading data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>64</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default receive buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP send buffer (SO_SNDBUF) to use when sending data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>128</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default send buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Request Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of time the client waits for the response of a request. If the response is not received before the timeout elapses, the client resends the request if necessary, or fails the request if the retries are exhausted. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>30</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Request Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Request Timeout</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default record limit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of records returned on a poll call to the Kafka cluster. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>500</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>DNS Lookups</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>DEFAULT</p></li><li><p>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</p></li><li><p>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how the client uses DNS lookups. If set to USE_ALL_DNS_IPS then, when the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, they attempt to connect before failing the connection. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers. If the value is RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p><div><p>When the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, a connection is attempted to all of the IP addresses before the connection fails. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers.</p></div></li><li><p><code>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</code></p><div><p>Each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Heartbeat interval</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Expected time between heartbeats to the consumer coordinator when using Kafkaâs group management facilities. Heartbeats are used to ensure that the consumerâs session stays active and to facilitate rebalancing when new consumers join or leave the group. The value must be set lower than <code>session.timeout.ms</code>, but typically should be set no higher than 1/3 of that value. It can be adjusted even lower to control the expected time for normal rebalances.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>3</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Heartbeat Interval Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Heartbeat interval</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Session Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Timeout used to detect consumer failures when using Kafkaâs group management facility. The consumer sends periodic heartbeats to indicate its aliveness to the broker. If no heartbeats are received by the broker before the expiration of this session timeout, then the broker removes this consumer from the group and initiates a rebalance. The value must be in the allowable range as configured in the broker configuration by <code>group.min.session.timeout.ms</code> and <code>group.max.session.timeout.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>10</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Session timeout time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Session Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection maximum idle time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Close idle connections after the number of milliseconds specified by this configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>540</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection maximum idle time time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Connection maximum idle time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Additional Properties</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Additional properties used to customize the Kafka connection.</p></div><div><p>For example:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>For serialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.serializer</code> or <code>value.serializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroSerializer</code>.</p></li><li><p>For deserialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.deserializer</code> or <code>value.deserializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>io.confluent.kafka.serializers.KafkaAvroDeserializer</code>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>TLS Configuration</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Tls">TLS</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines a TLS configuration used by both clients and server sides to secure the communication for the Mule app. The connector automatically sets the <code>security.protocol</code> to use for the communication. The valid values are <code>PLAINTEXT</code>, <code>SSL</code>, <code>SASL_PLAINTEXT</code>, or <code>SASL_SSL</code>. The default value is <code>PLAINTEXT</code> or <code>SASL_PLAINTEXT</code> for SASL authentication - kerberos/scram/plain. If the broker configures <code>SSL</code> as the protocol, then configure at least the keystore in the <code>tls:context</code> child element of the configuration and the connector automatically uses <code>SSL</code> (or <code>SASL_SSL</code> for SASL authentication) as the <code>security.protocol</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Endpoint identification algorithm</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Endpoint identification algorithm used by clients to validate the server hostname. The default value is an empty string, which means the endpoint identification algorithm is disabled. Clients, including client connections created by the broker for inter-broker communication, verify that the broker host name matches the host name in the brokers certificate.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Topic Subscription Patterns</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of subscription regular expressions to which to subscribe. Topics are automatically rebalanced between the topic consumers.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Assignments</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of <a href="#TopicPartition">Topic Partition</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of topic-partition pairs to assign. Consumers are not automatically rebalanced.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default fetch minimum size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Minimum amount of data the server returns for a fetch request. If insufficient data is available, the request waits for the specified minimum amount of data to accumulate before answering the request. The default setting of <code>1</code> byte means that fetch requests are answered as soon as a single byte of data is available or the fetch request times out waiting for data to arrive. If you set this to a value greater than <code>1</code>, the server waits for larger amounts of data to accumulate, which can improve server throughput slightly at the cost of some additional latency. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Minimum Size Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default fetch minimum size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>BYTE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default fetch maximum size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of data the server returns for a fetch request. Records are fetched in batches by the consumer. If the first record batch in the first non-empty partition of the fetch is larger than this value, the record batch is still returned to ensure that the consumer can make progress. As such, this is not an absolute maximum. The maximum record batch size accepted by the broker is defined using <code>message.max.bytes</code> (broker configuration) or <code>max.message.bytes</code> (topic configuration). The consumer performs multiple fetches in parallel. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum fetch size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default fetch maximum size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum partition fetch size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of data per partition that the server returns. The consumer fetches records in batches. If the first record batch in the first non-empty partition of the fetch is larger than this limit, the batch is still returned to ensure that the consumer can make progress. The maximum record batch size accepted by the broker is defined using <code>message.max.bytes</code> (broker configuration) or <code>max.message.bytes</code> (topic configuration). See <code>fetch.max.bytes</code> for limiting the consumer request size. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum partition fetch unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default maximum partition fetch size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Maximum Wait Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of time the server blocks before answering the fetch request if there isnât sufficient data to immediately satisfy the requirement specified by <code>fetch.min.bytes</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>500</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Maximum Wait Timeout Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Fetch Maximum Wait Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MILLISECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Reconnection</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Reconnection">Reconnection</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configures a reconnection strategy to use when a connector operation fails to connect to an external server.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Consumer Kerberos Connection Type

Use Kerberos configuration files.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Bootstrap Server URLs</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of servers to bootstrap the connection with the Kafka cluster. This can be a partial list of the available servers. For example, <code>localhost:9071</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Group ID</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Default group ID for all the Kafka consumers that use this configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Consumer Amount</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the number of consumers the connection initially creates.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum polling interval</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum delay between invocations of poll() when using consumer group management. This places an upper bound on the amount of time that the consumer can be idle before fetching more records. If poll() is not called before the expiration of this timeout, then the consumer is considered failed and the group rebalances to reassign the partitions to another member. For consumers using a non-null <code>group.instance.id</code> which reaches this timeout, partitions are not immediately reassigned. Instead, the consumer stops sending heartbeats and partitions are reassigned after the expiration of <code>session.timeout.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>60</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum Polling Interval Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Maximum polling interval</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Isolation Level</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>READ_UNCOMMITTED</p></li><li><p>READ_COMMITTED</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how to read messages that are written transactionally.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>READ_COMMITTED</code></p><div><p><code>consumer.poll()</code> returns only committed transactional messages are returned.</p></div></li><li><p><code>READ_UNCOMMITTED</code></p><div><p><code>consumer.poll()</code> returns all messages, even transactional messages that were aborted.</p></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Non-transactional messages are returned unconditionally in either mode. Messages are always returned in offset order. Hence, in the <code>READ_COMMITTED</code> mode, <code>consumer.poll()</code> returns only messages up to the last stable offset (LSO), which is one less than the offset of the first open transaction.</p></div><div><p>In particular, any messages appearing after messages belonging to ongoing transactions are withheld until the relevant transaction is completed. As a result, <code>READ_COMMITTED</code> consumers are not able to read up to the high watermark when there are in-flight transactions. Furthermore, when in <code>READ_COMMITTED</code>, the <code>seekToEnd</code> method returns the LSO.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>READ_UNCOMMITTED</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Exclude internal topics</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines whether internal topics matching a subscribed pattern are excluded from the subscription. It is always possible to explicitly subscribe to an internal topic.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>true</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Auto offset reset</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>EARLIEST</p></li><li><p>LATEST</p></li><li><p>ERROR</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines what to do when there is no initial offset in Kafka or if the current offset no longer exists on the server, for example, because the data was deleted.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>EARLIEST</p><div><p>Automatically reset the offset to the earliest offset.</p></div></li><li><p>LATEST</p><div><p>Automatically reset the offset to the latest offset.</p></div></li><li><p>ERROR</p><div><p>Throw an error if no previous offset is found for the consumerâs group.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>LATEST</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Amount of time to wait before attempting to retry a failed request to a given topic partition. This avoids repeatedly sending requests in a tight loop under some failure scenarios.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>100</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MILLISECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Check CRC</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Automatically check the CRC32 of the records consumed. This ensures that no on-the-wire or on-disk corruption to the messages occurred. This check adds some overhead, so in situations that require extremely high performance, this can be disabled.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>true</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP receive buffer (SO_RCVBUF) to use when reading data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>64</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default receive buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP send buffer (SO_SNDBUF) to use when sending data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>128</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default send buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Request Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of time the client waits for the response of a request. If the response is not received before the timeout elapses, the client resends the request if necessary, or fails the request if the retries are exhausted. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>30</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Request Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Request Timeout</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default record limit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of records returned on a poll call to the Kafka cluster. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>500</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>DNS Lookups</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>DEFAULT</p></li><li><p>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</p></li><li><p>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how the client uses DNS lookups. If set to USE_ALL_DNS_IPS then, when the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, they attempt to connect before failing the connection. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers. If the value is RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p><div><p>When the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, a connection is attempted to all of the IP addresses before the connection fails. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers.</p></div></li><li><p><code>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</code></p><div><p>Each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Heartbeat interval</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Expected time between heartbeats to the consumer coordinator when using Kafkaâs group management facilities. Heartbeats are used to ensure that the consumerâs session stays active and to facilitate rebalancing when new consumers join or leave the group. The value must be set lower than <code>session.timeout.ms</code>, but typically should be set no higher than 1/3 of that value. It can be adjusted even lower to control the expected time for normal rebalances.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>3</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Heartbeat Interval Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Heartbeat interval</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Session Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Timeout used to detect consumer failures when using Kafkaâs group management facility. The consumer sends periodic heartbeats to indicate its aliveness to the broker. If no heartbeats are received by the broker before the expiration of this session timeout, then the broker removes this consumer from the group and initiates a rebalance. The value must be in the allowable range as configured in the broker configuration by <code>group.min.session.timeout.ms</code> and <code>group.max.session.timeout.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>10</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Session timeout time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Session Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection maximum idle time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Close idle connections after the number of milliseconds specified by this configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>540</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection maximum idle time time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Connection maximum idle time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Additional Properties</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Additional properties used to customize the Kafka connection.</p></div><div><p>For example:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>For serialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.serializer</code> or <code>value.serializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroSerializer</code>.</p></li><li><p>For deserialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.deserializer</code> or <code>value.deserializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>io.confluent.kafka.serializers.KafkaAvroDeserializer</code>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>TLS Configuration</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Tls">TLS</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines a TLS configuration used by both clients and server sides to secure the communication for the Mule app. The connector automatically sets the <code>security.protocol</code> to use for the communication. The valid values are <code>PLAINTEXT</code>, <code>SSL</code>, <code>SASL_PLAINTEXT</code>, or <code>SASL_SSL</code>. The default value is <code>PLAINTEXT</code> or <code>SASL_PLAINTEXT</code> for SASL authentication - kerberos/scram/plain. If the broker configures <code>SSL</code> as the protocol, then configure at least the keystore in the <code>tls:context</code> child element of the configuration and the connector automatically uses <code>SSL</code> (or <code>SASL_SSL</code> for SASL authentication) as the <code>security.protocol</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Endpoint identification algorithm</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Endpoint identification algorithm used by clients to validate the server hostname. The default value is an empty string, which means the endpoint identification algorithm is disabled. Clients, including client connections created by the broker for inter-broker communication, verify that the broker host name matches the host name in the brokers certificate.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Topic Subscription Patterns</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of subscription regular expressions to which to subscribe. Topics are automatically rebalanced between the topic consumers.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Assignments</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of <a href="#TopicPartition">Topic Partition</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of topic-partition pairs to assign. Consumers are not automatically rebalanced.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default fetch minimum size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Minimum amount of data the server returns for a fetch request. If insufficient data is available, the request waits for the specified minimum amount of data to accumulate before answering the request. The default setting of <code>1</code> byte means that fetch requests are answered as soon as a single byte of data is available or the fetch request times out waiting for data to arrive. If you set this to a value greater than <code>1</code>, the server waits for larger amounts of data to accumulate, which can improve server throughput slightly at the cost of some additional latency. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Minimum Size Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default fetch minimum size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>BYTE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default fetch maximum size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of data the server returns for a fetch request. Records are fetched in batches by the consumer. If the first record batch in the first non-empty partition of the fetch is larger than this value, the record batch is still returned to ensure that the consumer can make progress. As such, this is not an absolute maximum. The maximum record batch size accepted by the broker is defined using <code>message.max.bytes</code> (broker configuration) or <code>max.message.bytes</code> (topic configuration). The consumer performs multiple fetches in parallel. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum fetch size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default fetch maximum size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum partition fetch size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of data per partition that the server returns. The consumer fetches records in batches. If the first record batch in the first non-empty partition of the fetch is larger than this limit, the batch is still returned to ensure that the consumer can make progress. The maximum record batch size accepted by the broker is defined using <code>message.max.bytes</code> (broker configuration) or <code>max.message.bytes</code> (topic configuration). See <code>fetch.max.bytes</code> for limiting the consumer request size. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum partition fetch unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default maximum partition fetch size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Maximum Wait Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of time the server blocks before answering the fetch request if there isnât sufficient data to immediately satisfy the requirement specified by <code>fetch.min.bytes</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>500</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Maximum Wait Timeout Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Fetch Maximum Wait Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MILLISECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Principal</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Entity that is authenticated by a computer system or a network.
