Principals can be individual people, computers, services, or computational entities such as processes and threads.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Service name</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Kerberos principal name that Kafka runs as.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Kerberos configuration file (krb5.conf)</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Path to the <code>krb5.conf</code> file, which contains Kerberos configuration information. This information includes the locations of KDCs and admin servers for the Kerberos realms of interest, defaults for the current realm, defaults for Kerberos applications, and the mappings of hostnames to Kerberos realms.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Use ticket cache</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Set this option to <code>true</code> to obtain the ticket-granting ticket (TGT) from the ticket cache. Set this option to <code>false</code> if you do not want to use the ticket cache. The connector searches for the ticket cache as follows:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>On Solaris and Linux, the connector looks in <code>/tmp/krb5cc_uid</code>, in which the <code>uid</code> is the numeric user identifier.</p></li><li><p>If the ticket cache is not available in <code>/tmp/krb5cc_uid</code> or the app is on a Windows platform, the connector looks in <code>{user.home}{file.separator}krb5cc_{user.name}</code>. You can override the ticket cache location by setting a value for the <strong>Ticket cache</strong> field.<br><br>In a Windows environment, if a ticket cannot be retrieved from the file ticket cache, Windows uses the Local Security Authority (LSA) API to get the ticket-granting ticket (TGT).</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>false</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Ticket cache</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Name of the ticket cache that contains the userâs ticket-granting ticket (TGT). If this value is set, <strong>Use ticket cache</strong> must also be set to <code>true</code>. Otherwise, a configuration error is returned.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Use keytab</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Set this option to <code>true</code> if you want the connector to obtain the principalâs key from the keytab. If you donât set this value, the connector locates the keytab by using the Kerberos configuration file. If the keytab is not specified in the Kerberos configuration file, the connector looks for the <code>{user.home}{file.separator}krb5.keytab</code> file.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>false</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Keytab</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Set this option to the file name of the keytab to obtain the principalâs secret key.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Store key</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Set this option to <code>true</code> to store the principalâs subject private credentials.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>false</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Reconnection</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Reconnection">Reconnection</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configures a reconnection strategy to use when a connector operation fails to connect to an external server.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Consumer SASL/OAUTHBEARER - Client Credentials Connection Type

OAuth Bearer authentication is a mechanism for authenticating requests using bearer tokens, which
are typically used to authenticate users and applications to access resources. Apache KafkaÂ®
supports OAuth Bearer authentication for establishing connections securely. Additionally, the
connector supports only
[RFC6749 standard](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6749#section-4.4.3).

For more information, refer to the
[Confluent](https://docs.confluent.io/platform/current/security/authentication/sasl/oauthbearer/overview.html)
documentation.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Bootstrap Server URLs</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of servers to bootstrap the connection with the Kafka cluster. This can be a partial list of the available servers. For example, <code>localhost:9071</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Endpoint identification algorithm</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The endpoint identification algorithm used by clients to validate server host name. The default value is an empty string, which means it is disabled. Clients including client connections created by the broker for inter-broker communication verify that the broker host name matches the host name in the brokers certificate.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Group ID</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Default Group ID for all the Kafka Consumers that use this configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Consumer Amount</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the number of consumers the connection initially creates.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum polling interval</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum delay between invocations of poll() when using consumer group management. This places an upper bound on the amount of time that the consumer can be idle before fetching more records. If poll() is not called before the expiration of this timeout, then the consumer is considered failed and the group rebalances to reassign the partitions to another member. For consumers using a non-null <code>group.instance.id</code> which reaches this timeout, partitions are not immediately reassigned. Instead, the consumer stops sending heartbeats and partitions are reassigned after the expiration of <code>session.timeout.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>60</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum Polling Interval Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for request timeout scalar. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>SECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Isolation Level</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>READ_UNCOMMITTED</p></li><li><p>READ_COMMITTED</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how to read messages that are written transactionally.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>READ_COMMITTED</code></p><div><p><code>consumer.poll()</code> returns only committed transactional messages are returned.</p></div></li><li><p><code>READ_UNCOMMITTED</code></p><div><p><code>consumer.poll()</code> returns all messages, even transactional messages that were aborted.</p></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Non-transactional messages are returned unconditionally in either mode. Messages are always returned in offset order. Hence, in the <code>READ_COMMITTED</code> mode, <code>consumer.poll()</code> returns only messages up to the last stable offset (LSO), which is one less than the offset of the first open transaction.</p></div><div><p>In particular, any messages appearing after messages belonging to ongoing transactions are withheld until the relevant transaction is completed. As a result, <code>READ_COMMITTED</code> consumers are not able to read up to the high watermark when there are in-flight transactions. Furthermore, when in <code>READ_COMMITTED</code>, the <code>seekToEnd</code> method returns the LSO.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>READ_UNCOMMITTED</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Exclude internal topics</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether internal topics matching a subscribed pattern is excluded from the subscription. It is always possible to explicitly subscribe to an internal topic.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>true</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Auto offset reset</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>EARLIEST</p></li><li><p>LATEST</p></li><li><p>ERROR</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines what to do when there is no initial offset in Kafka or if the current offset no longer exists on the server, for example, because the data was deleted.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>EARLIEST</p><div><p>Automatically reset the offset to the earliest offset.</p></div></li><li><p>LATEST</p><div><p>Automatically reset the offset to the latest offset.</p></div></li><li><p>ERROR</p><div><p>Throw an error if no previous offset is found for the consumerâs group.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>LATEST</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The amount of time to wait before attempting to retry a failed request to a given topic partition. This avoids repeatedly sending requests in a tight loop under some failure scenarios.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>100</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for the reconnect backoff timeout scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>MILLISECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Check CRC</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Automatically check the CRC32 of the records consumed. This ensures no on-the-wire or on-disk corruption to the messages occurred. This check adds some overhead, so it may be disabled in cases seeking extreme performance.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>true</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The size of the TCP receive buffer (SO_RCVBUF) to use when reading data. If the value is -1, the OS default is used. You can override this field at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>64</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The unit of measure for the receive buffer size scalar. You can override this field at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>KB</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The size of the TCP send buffer (SO_SNDBUF) to use when sending data. If the value is -1, the OS default is used. You can override this field at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>128</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The unit of measure for the send buffer size scalar. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>KB</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Request Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The configuration controls the maximum amount of time the client waits for the response of a request. If the response is not received before the timeout elapses the client will resend the request if necessary or fail the request if retries are exhausted. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>30</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Request Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for request timeout scalar. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>SECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default record limit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum number of records returned on a poll call to the Kafka cluster. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>500</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>DNS Lookups</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>DEFAULT</p></li><li><p>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</p></li><li><p>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how the client uses DNS lookups. If set to use_all_dns_ips then, when the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, they will all be attempted to connect to before failing the connection. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers. If the value is resolve_canonical_bootstrap_servers_only each entry will be resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Heartbeat interval</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The expected time between heartbeats to the consumer coordinator when using Kafkaâs group management facilities. Heartbeats are used to ensure that the consumerâs session stays active and to facilitate rebalancing when new consumers join or leave the group. The value must be set lower than session.timeout.ms, but typically should be set no higher than 1/3 of that value. It can be adjusted even lower to control the expected time for normal rebalances.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>3</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Heartbeat Interval Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for fetch heartbeat interval time scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>SECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Session timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The timeout used to detect consumer failures when using Kafkaâs group management facility. The consumer sends periodic heartbeats to indicate its liveness to the broker. If no heartbeats are received by the broker before the expiration of this session timeout, then the broker will remove this consumer from the group and initiate a rebalance. Note that the value must be in the allowable range as configured in the broker configuration by group.min.session.timeout.ms and group.max.session.timeout.ms.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>10</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Session timeout time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for session timeout scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>SECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection maximum idle time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Close idle connections after the number of milliseconds specified by this config.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>540</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection maximum idle time time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for connections maximum idle time scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>SECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Additional Properties</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Additional properties used to customize the Kafka connection.</p></div><div><p>For example:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>For serialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.serializer</code> or <code>value.serializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroSerializer</code>.</p></li><li><p>For deserialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.deserializer</code> or <code>value.deserializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>io.confluent.kafka.serializers.KafkaAvroDeserializer</code>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>TLS Configuration</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Tls">TLS</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines a TLS configuration, which can be used from both the client and server sides to secure communication for the Mule app. The connector will automatically set the 'security.protocol' to use for communication. Valid values are PLAINTEXT / SSL / SASL_PLAINTEXT / SASL_SSL. Default value when no configuration has been provided is PLAINTEXT(or SASL_PLAINTEXT for SASL authentication - kerberos/scram/plain/token). If SSL was configured as protocol on the broker side then the user needs to configure at least the keystore in the 'tls:context' child element of this config and the connector will automatically use SSL(or SASL_SSL for SASL authentication) as 'security.protocol'.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Topic Subscription Patterns</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The list of subscription regular expressions to subscribe to. This topics will be automatically rebalanced between the amount of consumers of the topic.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Assignments</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of <a href="#TopicPartition">Topic Partition</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The list of topic-partition pairs to assign. Note that there will be no automatic rebalance of the consumers</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default fetch minimum size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The minimum amount of data the server should return for a fetch request. If insufficient data is available the request waits for that much data to accumulate before answering the request. The default setting of 1 byte means that fetch requests are answered as soon as a single byte of data is available or the fetch request times out waiting for data to arrive. Setting this to something greater than 1 will cause the server to wait for larger amounts of data to accumulate which can improve server throughput a bit at the cost of some additional latency. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Minimum Size Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>BYTE</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default fetch maximum size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum amount of data the server should return for a fetch request. Records are fetched in batches by the consumer, and if the first record batch in the first non-empty partition of the fetch is larger than this value, the record batch will still be returned to ensure that the consumer can make progress. As such, this is not an absolute maximum. The maximum record batch size accepted by the broker is defined via message.max.bytes (broker config) or max.message.bytes (topic config). Note that the consumer performs multiple fetches in parallel. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum fetch size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The unit of measure for the maximum partition fetch size scalar. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>MB</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum partition fetch size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum amount of data per-partition the server will return. Records are fetched in batches by the consumer. If the first record batch in the first non-empty partition of the fetch is larger than this limit, the batch will still be returned to ensure that the consumer can make progress. The maximum record batch size accepted by the broker is defined via message.max.bytes (broker config) or max.message.bytes (topic config). See fetch.max.bytes for limiting the consumer request size.This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum partition fetch unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The unit of measure for the maximum partition fetch size scalar. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>MB</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Maximum Wait Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum amount of time the server will block before answering the fetch request if there isnât sufficient data to immediately satisfy the requirement given by fetch.min.bytes.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>500</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Maximum Wait Timeout Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for fetch maximum wait timeout scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>MILLISECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client ID Key</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the configuration key used to define the OAuth client ID within the JAAS configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>clientId</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client ID</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The value for the Client ID which refers to the unique identifier assigned to the OAuth client, facilitating secure authentication and authorization processes.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client Secret Key</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the configuration key used to define the OAuth client secret within the JAAS configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>clientSecret</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client Secret</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The value for the client secret which refers to the confidential authentication credential assigned to the OAuth client.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>OAuth Token Endpoint Key</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the configuration key used to define the OAuth token endpoint within the JAAS configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>sasl.oauthbearer.token.endpoint.url</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>OAuth Token Endpoint</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Describes the URL where clients can request access tokens from the authentication server.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Scope Key</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the configuration key used to define the OAuth scope within the JAAS configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>scope</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Scope</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines the specific permissions and access rights granted to the client application by the authorization server.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Audience Key</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the configuration key used to define the OAuth audience within the JAAS configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>audience</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Audience</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Comma separated list of one or more target audiences for the access token.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Max Token Expiration Seconds</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Sets the maximum duration, in seconds, for which an access token remains valid. This helps prevent unauthorized access by limiting the access tokenâs lifespan, ensuring that even if the access token is intercepted, it becomes invalid after a set period, thus enhancing security and enforcing re-authentication.</p></div><div><p>The default value is null (not set). When this field is not configured, the connector uses the actual token expiration time provided by the OAuth server in the token response (<code>expires_in</code> field). The token is valid for its full natural lifespan as determined by the authorization server. Leaving this field unset uses the OAuth serverâs intended token lifespan, balances security and performance, and follows best practices.</p></div><div><p>If this value is set longer than the actual token TTL, the token expires at its natural expiration time as provided by the OAuth server, not at the configured maximum. If this value is set to the same value as the actual token TTL, the token expires at the configured time, which happens to match the serverâs intended expiration time.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Principal Name</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unique identifier associated with an authenticated user, often used for authorization decisions and resource access.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Credentials Placement</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BASIC_AUTHENTICATION_HEADER</p></li><li><p>BODY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines whether to include client credentials in the basic authentication header or in the body of the authentication request.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>BASIC_AUTHENTICATION_HEADER</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>OAuth Module Required</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Indicates whether this login module must be successfully authenticated for Kafka clients to establish a connection using OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>true</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Include Accept Header</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to include 'Accept-application/json' header in the authentication request.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>true</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>OAuth Extensions</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Represents a mapping of key-value pairs containing extensions for custom broker OAuth implementations. If you specify a key for the OAuth Extensions field, ensure that the input contains only alphabetic characters and is at least one character long. If you specify a value for OAuth Extensions field, ensure the input contains only printable ASCII characters or whitespace (including space, tab, carriage return, and newline), and the value is at least one character long. You donât need to include the <code>extension_</code> part, such as in <code>extension_logicalCluster</code> or <code>extension_identityPoolId</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Login Refresh Window Factor</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time for which the login refresh thread waits until a certain portion of the credentialâs lifespan passes and attempts to refresh it. Acceptable values range from (0.5) 50% to (1.0) 100%, with a default of (0.8) 80% if unspecified.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.8</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Login Refresh Window Jitter</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum random jitter added to the login refresh threadâs sleep time relative to the credentialâs lifespan is between (0) 0% and (0.25) 25%, with a default of (0.05) 5% if unspecified.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.05</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Login Refresh Minimum Period</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The desired minimum time in seconds for the login refresh thread to wait before refreshing a credential. Legal values are between 0 and 900 (15 minutes).</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>60</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Login Refresh Buffer</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The amount of buffer time in seconds before credential expiration to maintain when refreshing a credential. Legal values are between 0 and 3600 (1 hour). A default value of 300 (5 minutes) is used if no value is specified.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>300</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Use Persistent Connections</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Indicates whether to use persistent connections:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>true</code></p></li></ul></div><div><p>Mule uses persistent connections.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>false</code></p></li></ul></div><div><p>Mule closes the connection after the first request completes.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>true</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Max Connections</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of connections to open to the backend. HTTP requests are sent in parallel over multiple connections. Setting this value too high can impact latency and consume additional resources without increasing throughput. By default, the number of connections is unlimited.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>-1</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection Idle Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The number of milliseconds that a connection can remain idle before it is closed. The value of this attribute is only used when persistent connections are enabled.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>30000</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Stream Response</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether received responses should be streamed, meaning processing continues as soon as all headers are parsed and the body streamed as it arrives. When enabled, the response MUST be eventually read since depending on the configured buffer size it may not fit into memory and processing will stop until space is available.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>false</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Response Buffer Size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the buffer that stores the HTTP response, in bytes. By default, the space is not limited.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>-1</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client Socket Properties</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#TcpClientSocketParams">Tcp Client Socket Params</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Encapsulates the configurable properties for TCP client socket connections.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Proxy Config Params</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#ProxyConfigParams">Proxy Config Params</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configures a proxy for outbound connections.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Follow Redirects</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies whether to follow redirects or not.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>false</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Response Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum time that the request element will block the execution of the flow waiting for the HTTP response.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>60000</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Reconnection</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Reconnection">Reconnection</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When the application is deployed, a connectivity test is performed on all connectors. If set to true, deployment fails if the test doesnât pass after exhausting the associated reconnection strategy</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Consumer SASL/SCRAM Connection Type

Use Salted Challenge Response Authentication Mechanism (SCRAM) or SASL/SCRAM, a family of SASL
mechanisms that addresses the security concerns with traditional mechanisms that perform username
and password authentication like PLAIN. Apache Kafka supports SCRAM-SHA-256 and SCRAM-SHA-512.

For more information, refer to the
[Confluent](https://docs.confluent.io/platform/current/security/authentication/sasl/scram/overview.html)
documentation.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Bootstrap Server URLs</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of servers to bootstrap the connection with the Kafka cluster. This can be a partial list of the available servers. For example, <code>localhost:9071</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Group ID</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Default group ID for all the Kafka consumers that use this configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Consumer Amount</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the number of consumers the connection initially creates.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum polling interval</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum delay between invocations of poll() when using consumer group management. This places an upper bound on the amount of time that the consumer can be idle before fetching more records. If poll() is not called before the expiration of this timeout, then the consumer is considered failed and the group rebalances to reassign the partitions to another member. For consumers using a non-null <code>group.instance.id</code> which reaches this timeout, partitions are not immediately reassigned. Instead, the consumer stops sending heartbeats and partitions are reassigned after the expiration of <code>session.timeout.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>60</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum Polling Interval Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Maximum polling interval</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Isolation Level</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>READ_UNCOMMITTED</p></li><li><p>READ_COMMITTED</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how to read messages that are written transactionally.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>READ_COMMITTED</code></p><div><p><code>consumer.poll()</code> returns only committed transactional messages are returned.</p></div></li><li><p><code>READ_UNCOMMITTED</code></p><div><p><code>consumer.poll()</code> returns all messages, even transactional messages that were aborted.</p></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Non-transactional messages are returned unconditionally in either mode. Messages are always returned in offset order. Hence, in the <code>READ_COMMITTED</code> mode, <code>consumer.poll()</code> returns only messages up to the last stable offset (LSO), which is one less than the offset of the first open transaction.</p></div><div><p>In particular, any messages appearing after messages belonging to ongoing transactions are withheld until the relevant transaction is completed. As a result, <code>READ_COMMITTED</code> consumers are not able to read up to the high watermark when there are in-flight transactions. Furthermore, when in <code>READ_COMMITTED</code>, the <code>seekToEnd</code> method returns the LSO.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>READ_UNCOMMITTED</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Exclude internal topics</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines whether internal topics matching a subscribed pattern are excluded from the subscription. It is always possible to explicitly subscribe to an internal topic.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>true</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Auto offset reset</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>EARLIEST</p></li><li><p>LATEST</p></li><li><p>ERROR</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines what to do when there is no initial offset in Kafka or if the current offset no longer exists on the server, for example, because the data was deleted.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>EARLIEST</p><div><p>Automatically reset the offset to the earliest offset.</p></div></li><li><p>LATEST</p><div><p>Automatically reset the offset to the latest offset.</p></div></li><li><p>ERROR</p><div><p>Throw an error if no previous offset is found for the consumerâs group.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>LATEST</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Amount of time to wait before attempting to retry a failed request to a given topic partition. This avoids repeatedly sending requests in a tight loop under some failure scenarios.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>100</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MILLISECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Check CRC</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Automatically check the CRC32 of the records consumed. This ensures that no on-the-wire or on-disk corruption to the messages occurred. This check adds some overhead, so in situations that require extremely high performance, this can be disabled.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>true</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP receive buffer (SO_RCVBUF) to use when reading data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>64</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default receive buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP send buffer (SO_SNDBUF) to use when sending data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>128</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default send buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Request Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of time the client waits for the response of a request. If the response is not received before the timeout elapses, the client resends the request if necessary, or fails the request if the retries are exhausted. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>30</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Request Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Request Timeout</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default record limit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of records returned on a poll call to the Kafka cluster. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>500</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>DNS Lookups</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>DEFAULT</p></li><li><p>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</p></li><li><p>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how the client uses DNS lookups. If set to USE_ALL_DNS_IPS then, when the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, they attempt to connect before failing the connection. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers. If the value is RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p><div><p>When the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, a connection is attempted to all of the IP addresses before the connection fails. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers.</p></div></li><li><p><code>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</code></p><div><p>Each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Heartbeat interval</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Expected time between heartbeats to the consumer coordinator when using Kafkaâs group management facilities. Heartbeats are used to ensure that the consumerâs session stays active and to facilitate rebalancing when new consumers join or leave the group. The value must be set lower than <code>session.timeout.ms</code>, but typically should be set no higher than 1/3 of that value. It can be adjusted even lower to control the expected time for normal rebalances.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>3</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Heartbeat Interval Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Heartbeat interval</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Session Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Timeout used to detect consumer failures when using Kafkaâs group management facility. The consumer sends periodic heartbeats to indicate its aliveness to the broker. If no heartbeats are received by the broker before the expiration of this session timeout, then the broker removes this consumer from the group and initiates a rebalance. The value must be in the allowable range as configured in the broker configuration by <code>group.min.session.timeout.ms</code> and <code>group.max.session.timeout.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>10</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Session timeout time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Session Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection maximum idle time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Close idle connections after the number of milliseconds specified by this configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>540</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection maximum idle time time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Connection maximum idle time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Additional Properties</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Additional properties used to customize the Kafka connection.</p></div><div><p>For example:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>For serialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.serializer</code> or <code>value.serializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroSerializer</code>.</p></li><li><p>For deserialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.deserializer</code> or <code>value.deserializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>io.confluent.kafka.serializers.KafkaAvroDeserializer</code>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>TLS Configuration</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Tls">TLS</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines a TLS configuration used by both clients and server sides to secure the communication for the Mule app. The connector automatically sets the <code>security.protocol</code> to use for the communication. The valid values are <code>PLAINTEXT</code>, <code>SSL</code>, <code>SASL_PLAINTEXT</code>, or <code>SASL_SSL</code>. The default value is <code>PLAINTEXT</code> or <code>SASL_PLAINTEXT</code> for SASL authentication - kerberos/scram/plain. If the broker configures <code>SSL</code> as the protocol, then configure at least the keystore in the <code>tls:context</code> child element of the configuration and the connector automatically uses <code>SSL</code> (or <code>SASL_SSL</code> for SASL authentication) as the <code>security.protocol</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Endpoint identification algorithm</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Endpoint identification algorithm used by clients to validate the server hostname. The default value is an empty string, which means the endpoint identification algorithm is disabled. Clients, including client connections created by the broker for inter-broker communication, verify that the broker host name matches the host name in the brokers certificate.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Topic Subscription Patterns</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of subscription regular expressions to which to subscribe. Topics are automatically rebalanced between the topic consumers.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Assignments</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of <a href="#TopicPartition">Topic Partition</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of topic-partition pairs to assign. Consumers are not automatically rebalanced.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default fetch minimum size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Minimum amount of data the server returns for a fetch request. If insufficient data is available, the request waits for the specified minimum amount of data to accumulate before answering the request. The default setting of <code>1</code> byte means that fetch requests are answered as soon as a single byte of data is available or the fetch request times out waiting for data to arrive. If you set this to a value greater than <code>1</code>, the server waits for larger amounts of data to accumulate, which can improve server throughput slightly at the cost of some additional latency. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Minimum Size Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default fetch minimum size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>BYTE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default fetch maximum size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of data the server returns for a fetch request. Records are fetched in batches by the consumer. If the first record batch in the first non-empty partition of the fetch is larger than this value, the record batch is still returned to ensure that the consumer can make progress. As such, this is not an absolute maximum. The maximum record batch size accepted by the broker is defined using <code>message.max.bytes</code> (broker configuration) or <code>max.message.bytes</code> (topic configuration). The consumer performs multiple fetches in parallel. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum fetch size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default fetch maximum size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum partition fetch size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of data per partition that the server returns. The consumer fetches records in batches. If the first record batch in the first non-empty partition of the fetch is larger than this limit, the batch is still returned to ensure that the consumer can make progress. The maximum record batch size accepted by the broker is defined using <code>message.max.bytes</code> (broker configuration) or <code>max.message.bytes</code> (topic configuration). See <code>fetch.max.bytes</code> for limiting the consumer request size. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default maximum partition fetch unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default maximum partition fetch size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Maximum Wait Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of time the server blocks before answering the fetch request if there isnât sufficient data to immediately satisfy the requirement specified by <code>fetch.min.bytes</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>500</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Maximum Wait Timeout Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Fetch Maximum Wait Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MILLISECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Username</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Username with which to login.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Password</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Password with which to login.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Encryption type</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>SHA256</p></li><li><p>SHA512</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Encryption algorithm used by SCRAM.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Reconnection</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Reconnection">Reconnection</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configures a reconnection strategy to use when a connector operation fails to connect to an external server.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Consumer SASL/PLAIN Connection Type

Use SASL authenticated with a username and password.

For more information, refer to the
[Confluent](https://docs.confluent.io/platform/current/security/authentication/sasl/plain/overview.html)
documentation.

Enable FIPS mode by setting the
`com.mulesoft.connectors.allow-registration-of-sasl-security-provider-in-fips=true` flag to true.
This adds the `com.sun.security.sasl.Provider` security provider in the FIPS environment.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Bootstrap Server URLs</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of servers to bootstrap the connection with the Kafka cluster. This can be a partial list of the available servers. For example, <code>localhost:9071</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Group ID</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Default group ID for all the Kafka consumers that use this configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Consumer Amount</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the number of consumers the connection initially creates.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum polling interval</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum delay between invocations of poll() when using consumer group management. This places an upper bound on the amount of time that the consumer can be idle before fetching more records. If poll() is not called before the expiration of this timeout, then the consumer is considered failed and the group rebalances to reassign the partitions to another member. For consumers using a non-null <code>group.instance.id</code> which reaches this timeout, partitions are not immediately reassigned. Instead, the consumer stops sending heartbeats and partitions are reassigned after the expiration of <code>session.timeout.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>60</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum Polling Interval Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Maximum polling interval</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Isolation Level</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>READ_UNCOMMITTED</p></li><li><p>READ_COMMITTED</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how to read messages that are written transactionally.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>READ_COMMITTED</code></p><div><p><code>consumer.poll()</code> returns only committed transactional messages are returned.</p></div></li><li><p><code>READ_UNCOMMITTED</code></p><div><p><code>consumer.poll()</code> returns all messages, even transactional messages that were aborted.</p></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Non-transactional messages are returned unconditionally in either mode. Messages are always returned in offset order. Hence, in the <code>READ_COMMITTED</code> mode, <code>consumer.poll()</code> returns only messages up to the last stable offset (LSO), which is one less than the offset of the first open transaction.</p></div><div><p>In particular, any messages appearing after messages belonging to ongoing transactions are withheld until the relevant transaction is completed. As a result, <code>READ_COMMITTED</code> consumers are not able to read up to the high watermark when there are in-flight transactions. Furthermore, when in <code>READ_COMMITTED</code>, the <code>seekToEnd</code> method returns the LSO.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>READ_UNCOMMITTED</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Exclude internal topics</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines whether internal topics matching a subscribed pattern are excluded from the subscription. It is always possible to explicitly subscribe to an internal topic.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>true</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Auto offset reset</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>EARLIEST</p></li><li><p>LATEST</p></li><li><p>ERROR</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines what to do when there is no initial offset in Kafka or if the current offset no longer exists on the server, for example, because the data was deleted.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>EARLIEST</p><div><p>Automatically reset the offset to the earliest offset.</p></div></li><li><p>LATEST</p><div><p>Automatically reset the offset to the latest offset.</p></div></li><li><p>ERROR</p><div><p>Throw an error if no previous offset is found for the consumerâs group.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>LATEST</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Amount of time to wait before attempting to retry a failed request to a given topic partition. This avoids repeatedly sending requests in a tight loop under some failure scenarios.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>100</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MILLISECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Check CRC</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Automatically check the CRC32 of the records consumed. This ensures that no on-the-wire or on-disk corruption to the messages occurred. This check adds some overhead, so in situations that require extremely high performance, this can be disabled.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>true</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP receive buffer (SO_RCVBUF) to use when reading data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>64</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default receive buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP send buffer (SO_SNDBUF) to use when sending data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>128</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default send buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Request Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of time the client waits for the response of a request. If the response is not received before the timeout elapses, the client resends the request if necessary, or fails the request if the retries are exhausted. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>30</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Request Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Request Timeout</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default record limit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of records returned on a poll call to the Kafka cluster. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>500</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>DNS Lookups</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>DEFAULT</p></li><li><p>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</p></li><li><p>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how the client uses DNS lookups. If set to USE_ALL_DNS_IPS then, when the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, they attempt to connect before failing the connection. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers. If the value is RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p><div><p>When the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, a connection is attempted to all of the IP addresses before the connection fails. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers.</p></div></li><li><p><code>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</code></p><div><p>Each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Heartbeat interval</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Expected time between heartbeats to the consumer coordinator when using Kafkaâs group management facilities. Heartbeats are used to ensure that the consumerâs session stays active and to facilitate rebalancing when new consumers join or leave the group. The value must be set lower than <code>session.timeout.ms</code>, but typically should be set no higher than 1/3 of that value. It can be adjusted even lower to control the expected time for normal rebalances.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>3</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Heartbeat Interval Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Heartbeat interval</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Session Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Timeout used to detect consumer failures when using Kafkaâs group management facility. The consumer sends periodic heartbeats to indicate its aliveness to the broker. If no heartbeats are received by the broker before the expiration of this session timeout, then the broker removes this consumer from the group and initiates a rebalance. The value must be in the allowable range as configured in the broker configuration by <code>group.min.session.timeout.ms</code> and <code>group.max.session.timeout.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>10</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Session timeout time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Session Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection maximum idle time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Close idle connections after the number of milliseconds specified by this configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>540</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection maximum idle time time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Connection maximum idle time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Additional Properties</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Additional properties used to customize the Kafka connection.</p></div><div><p>For example:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>For serialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.serializer</code> or <code>value.serializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroSerializer</code>.</p></li><li><p>For deserialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.deserializer</code> or <code>value.deserializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>io.confluent.kafka.serializers.KafkaAvroDeserializer</code>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>TLS Configuration</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Tls">TLS</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines a TLS configuration used by both clients and server sides to secure the communication for the Mule app. The connector automatically sets the <code>security.protocol</code> to use for the communication. The valid values are <code>PLAINTEXT</code>, <code>SSL</code>, <code>SASL_PLAINTEXT</code>, or <code>SASL_SSL</code>. The default value is <code>PLAINTEXT</code> or <code>SASL_PLAINTEXT</code> for SASL authentication - kerberos/scram/plain. If the broker configures <code>SSL</code> as the protocol, then configure at least the keystore in the <code>tls:context</code> child element of the configuration and the connector automatically uses <code>SSL</code> (or <code>SASL_SSL</code> for SASL authentication) as the <code>security.protocol</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Endpoint identification algorithm</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Endpoint identification algorithm used by clients to validate the server hostname. The default value is an empty string, which means the endpoint identification algorithm is disabled. Clients, including client connections created by the broker for inter-broker communication, verify that the broker host name matches the host name in the brokers certificate.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Topic Subscription Patterns</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of subscription regular expressions to which to subscribe. Topics are automatically rebalanced between the topic consumers.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Assignments</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of <a href="#TopicPartition">Topic Partition</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of topic-partition pairs to assign. Consumers are not automatically rebalanced.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default fetch minimum size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Minimum amount of data the server returns for a fetch request. If insufficient data is available, the request waits for the specified minimum amount of data to accumulate before answering the request. The default setting of <code>1</code> byte means that fetch requests are answered as soon as a single byte of data is available or the fetch request times out waiting for data to arrive. If you set this to a value greater than <code>1</code>, the server waits for larger amounts of data to accumulate, which can improve server throughput slightly at the cost of some additional latency. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Fetch Minimum Size Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default fetch minimum size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>BYTE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default fetch maximum size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of data the server returns for a fetch request. Records are fetched in batches by the consumer. If the first record batch in the first non-empty partition of the fetch is larger than this value, the record batch is still returned to ensure that the consumer can make progress. As such, this is not an absolute maximum. The maximum record batch size accepted by the broker is defined using <code>message.max.bytes</code> (broker configuration) or <code>max.message.bytes</code> (topic configuration). The consumer performs multiple fetches in parallel.
