Compression is of full batches of data, so the efficacy of batching will also impact the compression ratio (more batching means better compression).</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>NONE</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connections maximum idle time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Close idle connections after the value specified by this config.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>540</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connections maximum idle time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for the connections maximum idle scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>SECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Delivery timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>An upper bound on the time to report success or failure after a call to send() returns. This limits the total time that a record will be delayed prior to sending, the time to await acknowledgment from the broker (if expected), and the time allowed for retriable send failures. The producer may report failure to send a record earlier than this config if either an unrecoverable error is encountered, the retries have been exhausted, or the record is added to a batch which reached an earlier delivery expiration deadline. The value of this config should be greater than or equal to the sum of request.timeout.ms and linger.ms.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>120</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Delivery Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for the delivery timeout scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>SECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Enable idempotence</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When set to 'true', the producer will ensure that exactly one copy of each message is written in the stream. If 'false', producer retries due to broker failures, etc, may write duplicates of the retried message in the stream. Note that enabling idempotence requires max.in.flight.requests.per.connection to be less than or equal to 5, retries to be greater than 0 and acks must be 'all'. If these values are not explicitly set by the user, suitable values will be chosen. If incompatible values are set, a ConnectionException will be thrown</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>false</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Linger time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The producer groups together any records that arrive in between request transmissions into a single batched request. Normally this occurs only under load when records arrive faster than they can be sent out. However in some circumstances the client may want to reduce the number of requests even under moderate load. This setting accomplishes this by adding a small amount of artificial delay?that is, rather than immediately sending out a record the producer waits for up to the given delay to allow other records to be sent so that the sends can be batched together. This can be thought of as analogous to Nagleâs algorithm in TCP. This setting gives the upper bound on the delay for batching: once we get batch.size worth of records for a partition it is sent immediately regardless of this setting, however if we have fewer than this many bytes accumulated for this partition we will 'linger' for the specified time waiting for more records to show up. This setting defaults to 0 (i.e. no delay). Setting linger.ms=5, for example, would have the effect of reducing the number of requests sent but would add up to 5ms of latency to records sent in the absence of load.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Linger Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for the linger time scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>SECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum block time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The configuration controls how long KafkaProducer.send() and KafkaProducer.partitionsFor() will block.These methods can be blocked either because the buffer is full or metadata unavailable.Blocking in the user-supplied serializers or partitioner will not be counted against this timeout.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>60</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum block time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for the maximum block time scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>SECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum in flight requests</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum number of unacknowledged requests the client will send on a single connection before blocking. Note that if this setting is set to be greater than 1 and there are failed sends, there is a risk of message re-ordering due to retries (i.e., if retries are enabled).</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>5</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum request size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum size of a request in bytes. This setting will limit the number of record batches the producer will send in a single request to avoid sending huge requests. This is also effectively a cap on the maximum record batch size. Note that the server has its own cap on record batch size which may be different from this.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum request size unit.</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The unit of measure for the max request size scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>MB</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Producer acknowledge mode</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NONE</p></li><li><p>LEADER_ONLY</p></li><li><p>ALL</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The number of acknowledgments the producer requires the leader to have received before considering a request complete. This controls the durability of records that are sent</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>NONE</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The size of the TCP receive buffer (SO_RCVBUF) to use when reading data. If the value is -1, the OS default will be used. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>64</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The unit of measure for the receive buffer size scalar. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>KB</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retries amount</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Setting a value greater than zero will cause the client to resend any record whose send fails with a potentially transient error. Note that this retry is no different than if the client resent the record upon receiving the error. Allowing retries without setting max.in.flight.requests.per.connection to 1 will potentially change the ordering of records because if two batches are sent to a single partition, and the first fails and is retried but the second succeeds, then the records in the second batch may appear first. Note additionally that produce requests will be failed before the number of retries has been exhausted if the timeout configured by delivery.timeout.ms expires first before successful acknowledgment. Users should generally prefer to leave this config unset and instead use delivery.timeout.ms to control retry behavior.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for the retry backoff timeout time scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>MILLISECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry backoff timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The amount of time to wait before attempting to retry a failed request to a given topic partition. This avoids repeatedly sending requests in a tight loop under some failure scenarios.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>100</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The size of the TCP send buffer (SO_SNDBUF) to use when sending data. If the value is -1, the OS default will be used. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>128</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The unit of measure for the send buffer size scalar. This parameter can be overridden at source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>KB</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default request timeout time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the time unit for the request timeout time scalar.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>SECONDS</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default request timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The configuration controls the maximum amount of time the client waits for the response of a request. If the response is not received before the timeout elapses the client will resend the request if necessary or fail the request if retries are exhausted. This should be larger than replica.lag.time.max.ms (a broker configuration) to reduce the possibility of message duplication due to unnecessary producer retries.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>30</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Partitioner</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NONE</p></li><li><p>DEFAULT</p></li><li><p>ROUND_ROBIN</p></li><li><p>UNIFORM_STICKY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The configuration controls which partitioning strategy is used when sending messages without providing a key or a partition.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>DEFAULT</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Additional Properties</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Additional properties used to customize the Kafka connection.</p></div><div><p>For example:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>For serialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.serializer</code> or <code>value.serializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroSerializer</code>.</p></li><li><p>For deserialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.deserializer</code> or <code>value.deserializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>io.confluent.kafka.serializers.KafkaAvroDeserializer</code>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>TLS Configuration</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Tls">TLS</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines a TLS configuration, which can be used from both the client and server sides to secure communication for the Mule app. The connector will automatically set the 'security.protocol' to use for communication. Valid values are PLAINTEXT / SSL / SASL_PLAINTEXT / SASL_SSL. Default value when no configuration has been provided is PLAINTEXT(or SASL_PLAINTEXT for SASL authentication - kerberos/scram/plain/token). If SSL was configured as protocol on the broker side then the user needs to configure at least the keystore in the 'tls:context' child element of this config and the connector will automatically use SSL(or SASL_SSL for SASL authentication) as 'security.protocol'.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client ID Key</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the configuration key used to define the OAuth client ID within the JAAS configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>clientId</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client ID</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The value for the Client ID which refers to the unique identifier assigned to the OAuth client, facilitating secure authentication and authorization processes.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client Secret Key</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the configuration key used to define the OAuth client secret within the JAAS configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>clientSecret</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client Secret</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The value for the client secret which refers to the confidential authentication credential assigned to the OAuth client.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>OAuth Token Endpoint Key</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the configuration key used to define the OAuth token endpoint within the JAAS configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>sasl.oauthbearer.token.endpoint.url</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>OAuth Token Endpoint</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Describes the URL where clients can request access tokens from the authentication server.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Scope Key</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the configuration key used to define the OAuth scope within the JAAS configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>scope</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Scope</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines the specific permissions and access rights granted to the client application by the authorization server.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Audience Key</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the configuration key used to define the OAuth audience within the JAAS configuration.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>audience</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Audience</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Comma separated list of one or more target audiences for the access token.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Max Token Expiration Seconds</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Sets the maximum duration, in seconds, for which an access token remains valid. This helps prevent unauthorized access by limiting the access tokenâs lifespan, ensuring that even if the access token is intercepted, it becomes invalid after a set period, thus enhancing security and enforcing re-authentication.</p></div><div><p>The default value is null (not set). When this field is not configured, the connector uses the actual token expiration time provided by the OAuth server in the token response (<code>expires_in</code> field). The token is valid for its full natural lifespan as determined by the authorization server. Leaving this field unset uses the OAuth serverâs intended token lifespan, balances security and performance, and follows best practices.</p></div><div><p>If this value is set longer than the actual token TTL, the token expires at its natural expiration time as provided by the OAuth server, not at the configured maximum. If this value is set to the same value as the actual token TTL, the token expires at the configured time, which happens to match the serverâs intended expiration time.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Principal Name</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unique identifier associated with an authenticated user, often used for authorization decisions and resource access.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Credentials Placement</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BASIC_AUTHENTICATION_HEADER</p></li><li><p>BODY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines whether to include client credentials in the basic authentication header or in the body of the authentication request.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>BASIC_AUTHENTICATION_HEADER</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>OAuth Module Required</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Indicates whether this login module must be successfully authenticated for Kafka clients to establish a connection using OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>true</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Include Accept Header</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to include 'Accept-application/json' header in the authentication request.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>true</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>OAuth Extensions</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Represents a mapping of key-value pairs containing extensions for custom broker OAuth implementations. If you specify a key for the OAuth Extensions field, ensure that the input contains only alphabetic characters and is at least one character long. If you specify a value for OAuth Extensions field, ensure the input contains only printable ASCII characters or whitespace (including space, tab, carriage return, and newline), and the value is at least one character long. You donât need to include the <code>extension_</code> part, such as in <code>extension_logicalCluster</code> or <code>extension_identityPoolId</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Login Refresh Window Factor</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time for which the login refresh thread waits until a certain portion of the credentialâs lifespan passes and attempts to refresh it. Acceptable values range from (0.5) 50% to (1.0) 100%, with a default of (0.8) 80% if unspecified.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.8</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Login Refresh Window Jitter</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum random jitter added to the login refresh threadâs sleep time relative to the credentialâs lifespan is between (0) 0% and (0.25) 25%, with a default of (0.05) 5% if unspecified.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.05</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Login Refresh Minimum Period</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The desired minimum time in seconds for the login refresh thread to wait before refreshing a credential. Legal values are between 0 and 900 (15 minutes).</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>60</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Login Refresh Buffer</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The amount of buffer time in seconds before credential expiration to maintain when refreshing a credential. Legal values are between 0 and 3600 (1 hour). A default value of 300 (5 minutes) is used if no value is specified.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>300</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Use Persistent Connections</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Indicates whether to use persistent connections:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>true</code></p></li></ul></div><div><p>Mule uses persistent connections.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>false</code></p></li></ul></div><div><p>Mule closes the connection after the first request completes.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>true</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Max Connections</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of connections to open to the backend. HTTP requests are sent in parallel over multiple connections. Setting this value too high can impact latency and consume additional resources without increasing throughput. By default, the number of connections is unlimited.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>-1</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connection Idle Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The number of milliseconds that a connection can remain idle before it is closed. The value of this attribute is only used when persistent connections are enabled.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>30000</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Stream Response</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether received responses should be streamed, meaning processing continues as soon as all headers are parsed and the body streamed as it arrives. When enabled, the response MUST be eventually read since depending on the configured buffer size it may not fit into memory and processing will stop until space is available.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>false</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Response Buffer Size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the buffer that stores the HTTP response, in bytes. By default, the space is not limited.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>-1</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client Socket Properties</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#TcpClientSocketParams">Tcp Client Socket Params</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Encapsulates the configurable properties for TCP client socket connections.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Proxy Config Params</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#ProxyConfigParams">Proxy Config Params</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configures a proxy for outbound connections.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Follow Redirects</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies whether to follow redirects or not.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>false</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Response Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum time that the request element will block the execution of the flow waiting for the HTTP response.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>60000</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Reconnection</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Reconnection">Reconnection</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When the application is deployed, a connectivity test is performed on all connectors. If set to true, deployment fails if the test doesnât pass after exhausting the associated reconnection strategy</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Producer SASL/SCRAM Connection Type

Use Salted Challenge Response Authentication Mechanism (SCRAM) or SASL/SCRAM, a family of SASL
mechanisms that addresses the security concerns with traditional mechanisms that perform username
and password authentication like PLAIN. Apache Kafka supports SCRAM-SHA-256 and SCRAM-SHA-512.

For more information, refer to the
[Confluent](https://docs.confluent.io/platform/current/security/authentication/sasl/scram/overview.html)
documentation.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Bootstrap Server URLs</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of servers to bootstrap the connection with the Kafka cluster. This can be a partial list of the available servers. For example, <code>localhost:9071</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Batch size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Producer attempts to batch records together into fewer requests whenever multiple records are sent to the same partition. This helps performance on both the client and the server. This configuration controls the default batch size in bytes. No attempt is made to batch records larger than this size. Requests sent to brokers contain multiple batches, one for each partition with the data that is available to send. A small batch size makes batching less common and can reduce throughput (a batch size of <code>0</code> disables batching entirely). A very large batch size can result in more wasteful use of memory as a buffer of the specified batch size is always allocated in anticipation of additional records.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>16</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>The batch size unit of measure.</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Batch size</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Total bytes of memory the producer uses to buffer records waiting to send to the server. If records are sent faster than they are delivered to the server, the producer blocks for <code>max.block.ms</code>, after which it throws an exception. This setting generally corresponds to the total memory the producer uses, but is not exact because the memory used by the producer is not all used for buffering. Some additional memory is used for compression (if compression is enabled), as well as for maintaining in-flight requests. The default value in the Apache Kafka documentation is <code>33554432</code> (32MB), but this should be capped to align with expected values for Mule instances in CloudHub (v0.1 core).</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>The buffer memory size unit of measure.</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Buffer size</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>DNS Lookups</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>DEFAULT</p></li><li><p>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</p></li><li><p>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how the client uses DNS lookups. If set to USE_ALL_DNS_IPS then, when the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, they attempt to connect before failing the connection. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers. If the value is RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p><div><p>When the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, a connection is attempted to all of the IP addresses before the connection fails. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers.</p></div></li><li><p><code>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</code></p><div><p>Each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Compression type</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NONE</p></li><li><p>GZIP</p></li><li><p>SNAPPY</p></li><li><p>LZ4</p></li><li><p>ZSTD</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Compression type for all data generated by the producer. The default is <code>NONE</code> (no compression). Compression works on full batches of data, so the efficacy of batching also impacts the compression ratio. More batching means better compression.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>NONE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connections maximum idle time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Close idle connections after the specified time is reached.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>540</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connections maximum idle time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Connections maximum idle time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Delivery Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Upper limit on the time to report success or failure after a call to <code>send()</code> returns. This limits the total time that a record is delayed prior to sending, the time to await acknowledgment from the broker (if expected), and the time allowed for retrying send failures. The producer might report failure to send a record earlier than this configuration if either an unrecoverable error is encountered, the retries have been exhausted, or the record is added to a batch that reached an earlier delivery expiration deadline. The value of this configuration must be greater than or equal to the sum of <code>request.timeout.ms</code> and <code>linger.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>120</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Delivery Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Delivery Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Enable idempotence</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, the producer ensures that exactly one copy of each message is written in the stream. If <code>false</code>, the producer retries due to broker failures and might write duplicates of the retried message in the stream. Enabling idempotence requires <code>max.in.flight.requests.per.connection</code> to be less than or equal to <code>5</code>, retries to be greater than <code>0</code>. and acks to be <code>all</code>. If these values are not explicitly set by the user, suitable values are chosen. If incompatible values are set, a <code>ConnectionException</code> is thrown.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>false</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Linger time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Producer groups together any records that arrive in between request transmissions into a single batched request. Normally this occurs only under load when records arrive faster than they are sent out. However, in some circumstances the client might want to reduce the number of requests, even under moderate load.<br><br>This setting accomplishes this by adding a small amount of artificial delay (rather than immediately sending out a record the producer waits for up to the given delay to allow other records to be sent so that the sends can be batched together). This is analogous to Nagleâs algorithm in TCP. This setting gives the upper bound on the delay for batching.<br><br>After the specified <code>batch.size</code> worth of records is received for a partition, it is sent immediately regardless of this setting, however if fewer than the specified number of bytes accumulated for this partition is received, the producer "lingers" for the specified time waiting for more records to arrive.<br><br>This setting defaults to <code>0</code> (no delay). Setting <code>linger.ms=5</code>, for example, has the effect of reducing the number of requests sent but would add up to 5ms of latency to records sent in the absence of load.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>0</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Linger Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Linger time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum block time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls for how long <code>KafkaProducer.send()</code> and <code>KafkaProducer.partitionsFor()</code> are blocked. These methods can be blocked either because the buffer is full or the metadata <code>unavailable.Blocking</code> in the user-supplied serializers or partitioner is not counted against this timeout.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>60</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum block time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Maximum block time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum in flight requests</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of unacknowledged requests the client sends on a single connection before blocking. If the value is greater than <code>1</code> and there are failed sends, there is a risk of message re-ordering due to retries (if retries are enabled).</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>5</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum request size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum size of a request in bytes. This setting limits the number of record batches the producer sends in a single request to avoid sending huge requests. This is also effectively a cap on the maximum record batch size. Note that the server has its own cap on record batch size, which might be different from this.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum request size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Maximum request size</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Producer Acknowledge Mode</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NONE</p></li><li><p>LEADER_ONLY</p></li><li><p>ALL</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Number of acknowledgments the producer requires the leader to receive before considering a request complete. This controls the durability of records that are sent.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>NONE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP receive buffer (SO_RCVBUF) to use when reading data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>64</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default receive buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retries amount</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Setting a value greater than <code>0</code> causes the client to resend any record whose send fails with a potentially transient error. Note that this retry is no different than if the client resent the record upon receiving the error. Allowing retries without setting <code>max.in.flight.requests.per.connection</code> to <code>1</code> potentially changes the ordering of records because if two batches are sent to a single partition, and the first fails and is retried but the second succeeds, then the records in the second batch might appear first. Additionally, produce requests will be failed before the number of retries has been exhausted if the timeout configured by <code>delivery.timeout.ms</code> expires before successful acknowledgment. It is recommended that users leave this configuration unset and instead use <code>delivery.timeout.ms</code> to control retry behavior.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Amount of time to wait before attempting to retry a failed request to a given topic partition. This avoids repeatedly sending requests in a tight loop under some failure scenarios.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>100</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MILLISECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP send buffer (SO_SNDBUF) to use when sending data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>128</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default send buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default request timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of time the client waits for the response to a request. If the response is not received before the timeout elapses, the client resends the request if necessary or fails the request if retries are exhausted. This value must be larger than <code>replica.lag.time.max.ms</code> (a broker configuration) to reduce the possibility of message duplication due to unnecessary producer retries.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>30</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default request timeout time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Default request timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Partitioner</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NONE</p></li><li><p>DEFAULT</p></li><li><p>ROUND_ROBIN</p></li><li><p>UNIFORM_STICKY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls the partitioning strategy.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>DEFAULT</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Additional Properties</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Additional properties used to customize the Kafka connection.</p></div><div><p>For example:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>For serialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.serializer</code> or <code>value.serializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroSerializer</code>.</p></li><li><p>For deserialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.deserializer</code> or <code>value.deserializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>io.confluent.kafka.serializers.KafkaAvroDeserializer</code>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>TLS Configuration</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Tls">TLS</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines a TLS configuration, used by both clients and server sides to secure the communication for the Mule app. The connector automatically sets the <code>security.protocol</code> to use for the communication. The valid values are <code>PLAINTEXT</code>, <code>SSL</code>, <code>SASL_PLAINTEXT</code>, or <code>SASL_SSL</code>. If the broker configures <code>SSL</code> as the protocol, configure the keystore in the <code>tls:context</code> child element of the configuration and the connector will automatically use <code>SSL</code> (or <code>SASL_SSL</code> for SASL authentication) as the <code>security.protocol</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Endpoint identification algorithm</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Endpoint identification algorithm used by clients to validate the server hostname. The default value is an empty string, which means the endpoint identification algorithm is disabled. Clients, including client connections created by the broker for inter-broker communication, verify that the broker host name matches the host name in the brokers certificate.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Username</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Username with which to login.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Password</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Password with which to login.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>EncryptionType</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>SHA256</p></li><li><p>SHA512</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Encryption algorithm used by SCRAM.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Reconnection</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Reconnection">Reconnection</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configures a reconnection strategy to use when a connector operation fails to connect to an external server.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Producer SASL/PLAIN Connection Type

Use SASL authenticated with a username and password.

For more information, refer to the
[Confluent](https://docs.confluent.io/platform/current/security/authentication/sasl/plain/overview.html)
documentation.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Bootstrap Server URLs</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of servers to bootstrap the connection with the Kafka cluster. This can be a partial list of the available servers. For example, <code>localhost:9071</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Batch size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Producer attempts to batch records together into fewer requests whenever multiple records are sent to the same partition. This helps performance on both the client and the server. This configuration controls the default batch size in bytes. No attempt is made to batch records larger than this size. Requests sent to brokers contain multiple batches, one for each partition with the data that is available to send. A small batch size makes batching less common and can reduce throughput (a batch size of zero disables batching entirely). A very large batch size can result in more wasteful use of memory as a buffer of the specified batch size is always allocated in anticipation of additional records.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>16</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>The batch size unit of measure.</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Batch size</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Total bytes of memory the producer uses to buffer records waiting to send to the server. If records are sent faster than they are delivered to the server, the producer blocks for <code>max.block.ms</code>, after which it throws an exception. This setting generally corresponds to the total memory the producer uses, but is not exact because the memory used by the producer is not all used for buffering. Some additional memory is used for compression (if compression is enabled), as well as for maintaining in-flight requests. The default value in the Apache Kafka documentation is <code>33554432</code> (32MB), but this should be capped to align with expected values for Mule instances in CloudHub (v0.1 core).</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>The buffer memory size unit of measure.</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Buffer size</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>DNS Lookups</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>DEFAULT</p></li><li><p>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</p></li><li><p>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how the client uses DNS lookups. If set to USE_ALL_DNS_IPS then, when the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, they attempt to connect before failing the connection. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers. If the value is RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p><div><p>When the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, a connection is attempted to all of the IP addresses before the connection fails. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers.</p></div></li><li><p><code>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</code></p><div><p>Each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Compression type</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NONE</p></li><li><p>GZIP</p></li><li><p>SNAPPY</p></li><li><p>LZ4</p></li><li><p>ZSTD</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Compression type for all data generated by the producer. The default is <code>NONE</code> (no compression). Compression works on full batches of data, so the efficacy of batching also impacts the compression ratio. More batching means better compression.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>NONE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connections maximum idle time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Close idle connections after the specified time is reached.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>540</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connections maximum idle time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Connections maximum idle time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Delivery Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Upper limit on the time to report success or failure after a call to <code>send()</code> returns. This limits the total time that a record is delayed prior to sending, the time to await acknowledgment from the broker (if expected), and the time allowed for retrying send failures. The producer might report failure to send a record earlier than this configuration if either an unrecoverable error is encountered, the retries have been exhausted, or the record is added to a batch that reached an earlier delivery expiration deadline. The value of this configuration must be greater than or equal to the sum of <code>request.timeout.ms</code> and <code>linger.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>120</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Delivery Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Delivery Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Enable idempotence</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, the producer ensures that exactly one copy of each message is written in the stream. If <code>false</code>, the producer retries due to broker failures and might write duplicates of the retried message in the stream. Enabling idempotence requires <code>max.in.flight.requests.per.connection</code> to be less than or equal to <code>5</code>, retries to be greater than <code>0</code>. and acks to be <code>all</code>. If these values are not explicitly set by the user, suitable values are chosen. If incompatible values are set, a <code>ConnectionException</code> is thrown.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>false</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Linger time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Producer groups together any records that arrive in between request transmissions into a single batched request. Normally this occurs only under load when records arrive faster than they are sent out. However, in some circumstances the client might want to reduce the number of requests, even under moderate load.<br><br>This setting accomplishes this by adding a small amount of artificial delay (rather than immediately sending out a record the producer waits for up to the given delay to allow other records to be sent so that the sends can be batched together). This is analogous to Nagleâs algorithm in TCP. This setting gives the upper bound on the delay for batching.<br><br>After the specified <code>batch.size</code> worth of records is received for a partition, it is sent immediately regardless of this setting, however if fewer than the specified number of bytes accumulated for this partition is received, the producer "lingers" for the specified time waiting for more records to arrive.<br><br>This setting defaults to <code>0</code> (no delay). Setting <code>linger.ms=5</code>, for example, has the effect of reducing the number of requests sent but would add up to 5ms of latency to records sent in the absence of load.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>0</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Linger Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Linger time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum block time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls for how long <code>KafkaProducer.send()</code> and <code>KafkaProducer.partitionsFor()</code> are blocked. These methods can be blocked either because the buffer is full or the metadata <code>unavailable.Blocking</code> in the user-supplied serializers or partitioner is not counted against this timeout.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>60</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum block time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Maximum block time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum in flight requests</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of unacknowledged requests the client sends on a single connection before blocking. If the value is greater than <code>1</code> and there are failed sends, there is a risk of message re-ordering due to retries (if retries are enabled).</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>5</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum request size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum size of a request in bytes. This setting limits the number of record batches the producer sends in a single request to avoid sending huge requests. This is also effectively a cap on the maximum record batch size. Note that the server has its own cap on record batch size, which might be different from this.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum request size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Maximum request size</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Producer Acknowledge Mode</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NONE</p></li><li><p>LEADER_ONLY</p></li><li><p>ALL</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Number of acknowledgments the producer requires the leader to receive before considering a request complete. This controls the durability of records that are sent.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>NONE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP receive buffer (SO_RCVBUF) to use when reading data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>64</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default receive buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default receive buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retries amount</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Setting a value greater than <code>0</code> causes the client to resend any record whose send fails with a potentially transient error. Note that this retry is no different than if the client resent the record upon receiving the error. Allowing retries without setting <code>max.in.flight.requests.per.connection</code> to <code>1</code> potentially changes the ordering of records because if two batches are sent to a single partition, and the first fails and is retried but the second succeeds, then the records in the second batch might appear first. Additionally, produce requests will be failed before the number of retries has been exhausted if the timeout configured by <code>delivery.timeout.ms</code> expires before successful acknowledgment. It is recommended that users leave this configuration unset and instead use <code>delivery.timeout.ms</code> to control retry behavior.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Amount of time to wait before attempting to retry a failed request to a given topic partition. This avoids repeatedly sending requests in a tight loop under some failure scenarios.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>100</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Retry Backoff Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Retry Backoff Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>MILLISECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Size of the TCP send buffer (SO_SNDBUF) to use when sending data. If the value is <code>-1</code>, the OS default is used. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>128</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default send buffer size unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Default send buffer size</strong> field. You can override this parameter at the source level.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default request timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum amount of time the client waits for the response to a request. If the response is not received before the timeout elapses, the client resends the request if necessary or fails the request if retries are exhausted. This value must be larger than <code>replica.lag.time.max.ms</code> (a broker configuration) to reduce the possibility of message duplication due to unnecessary producer retries.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>30</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default request timeout time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Default request timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Partitioner</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NONE</p></li><li><p>DEFAULT</p></li><li><p>ROUND_ROBIN</p></li><li><p>UNIFORM_STICKY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls the partitioning strategy.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>DEFAULT</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Additional Properties</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Additional properties used to customize the Kafka connection.</p></div><div><p>For example:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>For serialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.serializer</code> or <code>value.serializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>com.mulesoft.connectors.kafka.internal.model.serializer.MuleKafkaAvroSerializer</code>.</p></li><li><p>For deserialization, set <strong>Key</strong> to <code>key.deserializer</code> or <code>value.deserializer</code> and <strong>Value</strong> to <code>io.confluent.kafka.serializers.KafkaAvroDeserializer</code>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>TLS Configuration</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Tls">TLS</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Defines a TLS configuration, used by both clients and server sides to secure the communication for the Mule app. The connector automatically sets the <code>security.protocol</code> to use for the communication. The valid values are <code>PLAINTEXT</code>, <code>SSL</code>, <code>SASL_PLAINTEXT</code>, or <code>SASL_SSL</code>. If the broker configures <code>SSL</code> as the protocol, configure the keystore in the <code>tls:context</code> child element of the configuration and the connector will automatically use <code>SSL</code> (or <code>SASL_SSL</code> for SASL authentication) as the <code>security.protocol</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Endpoint identification algorithm</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Endpoint identification algorithm used by clients to validate the server hostname. The default value is an empty string, which means the endpoint identification algorithm is disabled. Clients, including client connections created by the broker for inter-broker communication, verify that the broker host name matches the host name in the brokers certificate.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Username</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Username with which to login.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Password</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Password with which to login.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Reconnection</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Reconnection">Reconnection</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configures a reconnection strategy to use when a connector operation fails to connect to an external server.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Producer SASL/TOKEN Connection Type

Use delegation tokens to authenticate to the Kafka cluster.

> [!NOTE] Due to security reasons, a delegation token cannot be renewed if the initial
> authentication uses a delegation token. For more information, refer to
> [Delegation Token Support for Kafka](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/KIP-48+Delegation+token+support+for+Kafka).

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Bootstrap Server URLs</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>List of servers to bootstrap the connection with the Kafka cluster. This can be a partial list of the available servers. For example, <code>localhost:9071</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td><td><div><div><p>x</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Batch size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Producer attempts to batch records together into fewer requests whenever multiple records are sent to the same partition. This helps performance on both the client and the server. This configuration controls the default batch size in bytes. No attempt is made to batch records larger than this size. Requests sent to brokers contain multiple batches, one for each partition with the data that is available to send. A small batch size makes batching less common and can reduce throughput (a batch size of zero disables batching entirely). A very large batch size can result in more wasteful use of memory as a buffer of the specified batch size is always allocated in anticipation of additional records.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>16</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>The batch size unit of measure.</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Batch size</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Buffer size</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Total bytes of memory the producer uses to buffer records waiting to send to the server. If records are sent faster than they are delivered to the server, the producer blocks for <code>max.block.ms</code>, after which it throws an exception. This setting generally corresponds to the total memory the producer uses, but is not exact because the memory used by the producer is not all used for buffering. Some additional memory is used for compression (if compression is enabled), as well as for maintaining in-flight requests. The default value in the Apache Kafka documentation is <code>33554432</code> (32MB), but this should be capped to align with expected values for Mule instances in CloudHub (v0.1 core).</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>The buffer memory size unit of measure.</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Unit of measure for the <strong>Buffer size</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>KB</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>DNS Lookups</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>DEFAULT</p></li><li><p>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</p></li><li><p>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls how the client uses DNS lookups. If set to USE_ALL_DNS_IPS then, when the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, they attempt to connect before failing the connection. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers. If the value is RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p><div><p>When the lookup returns multiple IP addresses for a hostname, a connection is attempted to all of the IP addresses before the connection fails. Applies to both bootstrap and advertised servers.</p></div></li><li><p><code>RESOLVE_CANONICAL_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_ONLY</code></p><div><p>Each entry is resolved and expanded into a list of canonical names.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>USE_ALL_DNS_IPS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Compression type</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NONE</p></li><li><p>GZIP</p></li><li><p>SNAPPY</p></li><li><p>LZ4</p></li><li><p>ZSTD</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Compression type for all data generated by the producer. The default is <code>NONE</code> (no compression). Compression works on full batches of data, so the efficacy of batching also impacts the compression ratio. More batching means better compression.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>NONE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connections maximum idle time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Close idle connections after the specified time is reached.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>540</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Connections maximum idle time unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Connections maximum idle time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Delivery Timeout</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Upper limit on the time to report success or failure after a call to <code>send()</code> returns. This limits the total time that a record is delayed prior to sending, the time to await acknowledgment from the broker (if expected), and the time allowed for retrying send failures. The producer might report failure to send a record earlier than this configuration if either an unrecoverable error is encountered, the retries have been exhausted, or the record is added to a batch that reached an earlier delivery expiration deadline. The value of this configuration must be greater than or equal to the sum of <code>request.timeout.ms</code> and <code>linger.ms</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>120</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Delivery Timeout Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Delivery Timeout</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Enable idempotence</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, the producer ensures that exactly one copy of each message is written in the stream. If <code>false</code>, the producer retries due to broker failures and might write duplicates of the retried message in the stream. Enabling idempotence requires <code>max.in.flight.requests.per.connection</code> to be less than or equal to <code>5</code>, retries to be greater than <code>0</code>. and acks to be <code>all</code>. If these values are not explicitly set by the user, suitable values are chosen. If incompatible values are set, a <code>ConnectionException</code> is thrown.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>false</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Linger time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Producer groups together any records that arrive in between request transmissions into a single batched request. Normally this occurs only under load when records arrive faster than they are sent out. However, in some circumstances the client might want to reduce the number of requests, even under moderate load.<br><br>This setting accomplishes this by adding a small amount of artificial delay (rather than immediately sending out a record the producer waits for up to the given delay to allow other records to be sent so that the sends can be batched together). This is analogous to Nagleâs algorithm in TCP. This setting gives the upper bound on the delay for batching.<br><br>After the specified <code>batch.size</code> worth of records is received for a partition, it is sent immediately regardless of this setting, however if fewer than the specified number of bytes accumulated for this partition is received, the producer "lingers" for the specified time waiting for more records to arrive.<br><br>This setting defaults to <code>0</code> (no delay). Setting <code>linger.ms=5</code>, for example, has the effect of reducing the number of requests sent but would add up to 5ms of latency to records sent in the absence of load.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>0</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Linger Time Unit</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit for the <strong>Linger time</strong> field.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>SECONDS</code></p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Maximum block time</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Controls for how long <code>KafkaProducer.send()</code> and <code>KafkaProducer.partitionsFor()</code> are blocked.
